verdict.evidence:
Re-ran https://stellarlight.xyz/api/repos/search?q=game&limit=31 (2026-07-09, total=31). Every positional fact reproduces exactly: top-10 is 100% Feb-2026 'Stellar Hacks: ZK Gaming' submissions; the byte-identical description "A development tool for building web3 games on Stellar" appears on FIVE rows in the top-11 — julianclatro #1 (isFork:true, repoScore 44), Mackenzie-OO7 #7 (fork, 26), Alisson-tech #9 (23), devfelipenunes #10 (23), Vl-Tatarinov/Stellar-Game-Studio_sea_battle #11 (20). litemint/cyberbrawl-community-resources (scfAwarded:true, topic 'game') is #13; xBull-Corp/Guess-the-xBull is #21 with codeVerified {stellarProof:'cargo-sdk', codeDepth:0.557} exactly as claimed. Non-Stellar rows confirmed: gaudiatech/pyved-engine #12 ("a pythonic modern game engine...built around pygame", zero Stellar signal in desc/topics, judgedHackathon null) and vottundev/qubic-game-proposal #17 (empty description, repoScore 7, lastCommit 2024-11).

Fairness check passes — this is not taste about ordering among equally-valid results. The response's own meta note states the contract: "Code references graded by repoScore (0-100)... Lead with high-score repos as the strongest existing references." Observed top-10 spans repoScore 23-44 while repoScore 74 (Cubeathon) sits at #18, 81 (Cosmic-Coder) at #25, and repoScore-7 qubic-game-proposal at #17 outranks both — the score:5/score:3 match-tier fully dominates the advertised quality grading, and within score:5 ordering doesn't follow repoScore either (#12 repoScore 54 below #11 repoScore 20). Serving 5 clones of one forked template in the top-11 is a dedup failure regardless of ranking philosophy (only the best-judged clone, julianclatro judgeScore 0.72, has any claim to a slot).

NEW strengthening evidence: fredericrezeau/xray-games — the ZK Gaming hackathon WINNER (hackathonWinner:true, judgeScore 0.99, repoScore 84 'high', cargo-verified codeDepth 0.78, sdk 25.0.0) — is entirely ABSENT from q=game (total=31, checked all rows) while ranking #3 for q=gaming (total=6 confirmed). Its name/description use 'games'/'gaming', so no-stemming token match makes the corpus's single best gaming reference invisible to the most natural query. Secondary RECALL-MISS on the same cell.

One framing correction: Guess-the-xBull is itself a Feb-2026 ZK-hackathon submission (judgedHackathon:'Stellar Hacks: ZK Gaming', judgeScore 0.66), not a pre-existing established repo as the claim implies — but the inversion stands on its own data (cargo-verified, repoScore 58, ranked below empty-description repoScore-7/33 rows).

confirmed.observed: q=fuzzing total=0 while both fuzz-named rows exist in-corpus; q=gaming (6) vs q=game (31) shows the same stemming split on another vertical.

verdict.evidence: Re-ran all probes 2026-07-09 ~17:54Z. (1) /api/repos/search?q=fuzzing&limit=10 → {"counts":{"returned":0,"total":0},"repos":[]}. (2) ?q=fuzz → total=1, sole hit lightsail-network/app-stellar-fuzz-corpus ("Used for storing initialized corpus (app-stellar)", lastCommitAt 2025-11-10). (3) ?q=fuzzer → total=1, sole hit inferara/inference-fuzzer. Both fuzz-named repos exist in-corpus yet neither is retrieved for the natural query "fuzzing" — with total=0 this is absence, not a top-K ordering dispute, so the top-5 niche tolerance is trivially violated. Expectation fairness confirmed from the records' own data: both repo names literally contain the stem (fuzz-corpus, -fuzzer) and both are fuzzing artifacts by their own descriptions. Correct-but-different explanations checked and excluded: matching is literal-keyword (projects lane returns Gecko Fuzz for q=fuzzing only because its shortDescription contains the literal word "fuzzing"), so no stemming/prefix normalization exists in the repos lane; the query token "fuzzing" simply never equals doc tokens "fuzz"/"fuzzer". Secondary evidence also reproduces: repos q=gaming total=6 vs q=game total=31 (same unstemmed vocabulary split on another vertical). Sub-claim about gecko-fuzz verified too: /api/projects/search?q=gecko → curated Tooling project gecko-fuzz (links.github=https://github.com/jjjutla/GeckoFuzz) with "repos":[], and /api/repos/search?q=gecko returns only thor-gecko-terminal and a CoinGecko oracle-demo — the GeckoFuzz repo itself is absent from the repo index (note: that sliver is a repo-coverage gap rather than stemming, but it is stated accurately in the claim). Mitigation check: the projects lane surfacing Gecko Fuzz for q=fuzzing does not cure the repos-lane miss — a dev querying search_repos for fuzzing tooling gets an honest-looking but wrong 0 while two on-topic repos exist. RECALL-MISS is the correct label.

verdict.evidence: Re-ran all probes 2026-07-09T17:54Z; the claim reproduces exactly. (1) q=Secp256r1Signer → sole hit crossmint/stellar-smart-account (total=1, rank 0), whose codeVerified.symbols contains "Secp256r1Signer","Ed25519Signer","SignerKey","SignerRole","SignerPolicy" — so the record's own structured data unambiguously implies the query; expectation is fair. (2) q=secp256r1 → {"counts":{"returned":0,"total":0}} — confirmed 0 despite the in-corpus symbol. Also 0 for "Secp256r1" and "SECP256R1". (3) q=secp256r1%20signer → 16 results, crossmint at index 13 (score=4, behind 11 score=5 generic signer repos: albedo, getaxal/verified-signer, CubeSigner-Go-SDK, ... none of which have any secp256r1 evidence; their codeVerified is null/empty). Outside both the top-5 niche tolerance and top-10. (4) Decisive control: q=signer alone returns the IDENTICAL 16 repos in the IDENTICAL order — the "secp256r1" term is a complete no-op, silently dropped, so the only repo matching the discriminating term gets no boost at all. Not correct-but-different behavior: crossmint is the only on-topic answer (sole secp256r1 repo in corpus; q=passkey returns different repos, socket-fi, also not surfaced here). Mechanism refinement to the claim's wording: it is not pure case sensitivity — q=secp256r1signer (lowercase, joined) DOES match (total=1, crossmint rank 0), so symbol matching is case-insensitive exact-token; the failure is that the bare curve name "secp256r1" doesn't match as a sub-token/prefix of the CamelCase symbol, and unmatched terms in multi-word queries are silently ignored rather than scored. Minor claim typo: "Carrier ranks 13/16" should read "crossmint/stellar-smart-account ranks 13/16".
verdict.correctedMode: RECALL-MISS (label correct; standalone q=secp256r1 total=0 additionally exhibits EMPTY-DISHONEST as a secondary facet — silently dropped/unmatched term with a misleading 0-result response)

confirmed.expected: nethermindeth/stellar-private-payments in top-5: symbols include CircomGroth16VerifierInterface, and exact q=Groth16Verifier returns it as sole hit rank 0
confirmed.observed: Carrier absent from all 6 results; standalone q=groth16 returns total=0 despite Groth16* symbols existing in corpus. Contrast: snake_case digit token works (q=bn256%20modulus → carrier rank 0)

verdict.evidence:
Re-ran all probes 2026-07-09 against live API; every element of the claim reproduces, and adversarial contrast probes strengthen it.

1) Main probe reproduces: /api/repos/search?q=groth16%20verifier&limit=10 → counts {"returned":6,"total":6}; results = nethermindeth/stellar-risc0-verifier (score 5), boundless-xyz/stellar-risc0-verifier (5), xycloo/wasm-groth16-verifier (5), openzeppelin/stellar-contracts (4), Dprof-in-tech/stipend (4), soundnesslabs/sp1-sui (3). nethermindeth/stellar-private-payments is ABSENT — and since total=6 < limit=10 it is absent from the ENTIRE match set, not merely outside top-5, so the top-5 tolerance question is moot.

2) Expectation is fair, provable from the record's own data: q=Groth16Verifier → total=1, rank 0 = nethermindeth/stellar-private-payments, whose codeVerified.symbols include "CircomGroth16VerifierInterface" (full list confirmed: [..."ASPMembershipInterface","ASPNonMembershipInterface","CircomGroth16VerifierInterface",...]). The engine demonstrably indexes symbols and CAN answer this query class: q=bn256%20modulus → total=1, carrier rank 0 (claimed contrast confirmed); q=bn256 → total=1 carrier; even lowercase q=groth16verifier → total=1 carrier (so it is NOT a case-sensitivity quirk — it is a tokenization one: the concatenated substring matches, the natural space-separated tokens do not).

3) Sub-claim confirmed: q=groth16 → total=0, despite the Groth16 symbol above AND nethermindeth/stellar-risc0-verifier carrying literal topic "groth16".

4) Not correct-but-different behavior: q=verifier alone returns the identical 6 repos with identical scores (5,5,5,4,4,3) as q="groth16 verifier" — the groth16 token contributed zero matching signal; the 6-result set is purely the "verifier" match set, so the query was never actually processed as a groth16 query.

5) Class evidence (not a one-off): q=risc0 → total=0 despite two repos named stellar-risc0-verifier (one with literal topic "risc0"); q=Groth16 → total=0. Single tokens containing digits fail against camelCase symbols, repo-name tokens, and topics, while snake_case-delimited digit tokens (bn256) work.

Mode label RECALL-MISS is correct: the record's own structured data (symbols) implies the query, the engine provably indexes that data, and the record is absent from the full result set.

verdict.evidence: Re-ran https://stellarlight.xyz/api/repos/search?q=DidStellarRegistry&limit=13 (2026-07-09T17:54Z): reproduced exactly. Ranks 1-4 = xcapit/karma-registry (score 5, repoScore 30), withobsrvr/nomad-pack-web3-registry (5, 19), vialabs-io/npm-registry (5, 14), mojoflower-garden/CarbonRegistryGraph (5, 6) — all four codeVerified:null, zero symbols, no 'did'/'stellar' in name or description (two have no description at all); they match only the generic 'registry' fullName token. acta-team/contracts-acta at rank 6 (score 4, repoScore 60) is the ONLY corpus repo whose codeVerified.symbols contains the exact query string ("DidStellarRegistry","DidStellarRegistryInterface") and its description reads "did:stellar on-chain registry". Fails both top-5 and top-10 tolerance despite being the sole on-topic record. So symbol matching works only on the verbatim symbol string; the digit-segment sub-token "ed25519" contributes nothing, and the combined-query rank comes purely from "signer" — the claimed secp256r1/groth16 digit-segment tokenization class. Not correct-but-different behavior: the discriminating token is silently dropped and the query degrades to a generic signer search. Minor nits vs the claim text: the observed rank today is 14/16 not 13/16 (one-position shift), and the claim's "Carrier" is a wrong name for the expected repo (crossmint/stellar-smart-account); neither affects the finding. The q=ed25519 → 0-results behavior is an additional EMPTY-DISHONEST-flavored symptom of the same root cause. RECALL-MISS label is correct.

verdict.evidence:
REPRODUCED exactly: GET /api/repos/explain?q=how%20does%20soroban%20charge%20rent%20for%20contract%20storage returns {"repo":"winsznx/routedock","routedVia":"search","answered":false,"answer":null,"alternateRepos":[]}. routedock is a 4-star payments SDK ("Unified payment execution layer for Stellar x402, MPP charge, MPP session...") — it wins the search fallback via lexical collision on "charge" (MPP charge), and DeepWiki has no index for it, so the caller gets an off-topic repo and an empty answer. The expected target is fair and in-corpus: /api/repos/search?q=rs-soroban-env returns stellar/rs-soroban-env (repoScore 46, "Rust environment for Soroban contracts"); rs-soroban-sdk also present (repoScore 63).

ONE CORRECTION to the claim's observed detail: "No canonical trigger for rent/ttl/storage/host exists" is overbroad. The canonical layer DOES cover this topic partially — probes show q="how does soroban ttl work" → routedVia:"canonical" → stellar/rs-soroban-env, answered:true, and q="soroban state archival" → canonical → rs-soroban-env, answered:true. That is the system's own ground truth that this topic canonically belongs to rs-soroban-env, which strengthens the finding: the trigger vocabulary simply misses the natural synonyms. Fall-through phrasings all mis-route to junk via search: "soroban contract storage rent" → blaineheffron/soroban-sdk-tools (answered:false); "how does the soroban host environment work" → Marcos-sxt/le_coup (answered:false); "soroban storage" and "how much does soroban storage cost" → blaineheffron/soroban-sdk-tools (answered:false). "Rent" is legitimate in-domain vocabulary (Stellar's own state-archival docs use rent fees; cross-chain users ask it constantly), and explain is a top-1 contract (single repo, alternateRepos empty), so tolerance is not an excuse. Not explained by correct-but-different behavior: the canonical router demonstrably exists (q="how does stellar consensus work" → canonical → stellar/stellar-core) and already maps this exact topic for other phrasings.

Net: finding holds, narrowed — the rs-soroban-env canonical mapping exists but its trigger vocabulary misses rent/storage/storage-cost/host phrasings, and the search fallback then serves off-topic low-star repos with answered:false. Fix shape: add rent/storage/state-archival/host synonyms to the existing rs-soroban-env canonical trigger.
verdict.correctedMode: ROUTING-MISS (confirmed, but narrowed: canonical trigger for the topic EXISTS via ttl/state-archival phrasings — the gap is missing rent/storage/host synonym triggers; the resulting top-1 could equally be filed as WRONG-TOP since in-corpus rs-soroban-env is outranked by off-topic routedock)

confirmed.expected: stellar/stellar-core is the C++ p2p validator (its own description: 'Reference implementation for the peer-to-peer agent') — not a deployable Soroban contract
confirmed.observed: codeVerified block claims isDeployableContract:true, stellarProof:'cargo-sdk', sorobanSdkVersion:'=23.5.3' — the vendored soroban host crates are being misread as a deployable contract; this false structured field feeds downstream scoring/answers

verdict.evidence: CONFIRMED — the finding holds and is provable from the system's own contract, not opinion. (1) Probe reproduces exactly: GET https://stellarlight.xyz/api/repos/search?q=stellar-core&limit=1 returns stellar/stellar-core with codeVerified: {"stellarProof":"cargo-sdk","codeDepth":0.333,"isDeployableContract":true,"sorobanSdkVersion":"=23.5.3","symbols":[]}. (2) The expectation is fair by the API's OWN spec: /api/openapi.json defines isDeployableContract as "Cargo cdylib — a real deployable Soroban contract (vs a CLI/indexer/frontend that only uses Stellar)" and stellarProof cargo-sdk as "soroban-sdk dep". (3) Ground truth from the record's own repo refutes the field: stellar-core's workspace has exactly one Rust crate (src/rust/Cargo.toml) with crate-type=["staticlib"] — not cdylib — and its soroban deps are soroban-env-host / soroban-test-wasms (the embedded HOST, not a contract using soroban-sdk); no "=23.5.3" soroban-sdk pin exists in the current manifest, so that value is at best a stale/transitive lock-file read. The record's own description ("Reference implementation for the peer-to-peer agent") and even /api/repos/explain's own prose answer ("reference implementation of the Stellar network protocol... written in C++20") contradict the structured field served alongside them. (4) Downstream propagation confirmed: /api/repos/explain?q=stellar-core serves the identical false codeVerified block on the canonical routing lane. (5) Not correct-but-different behavior — the scanner demonstrably can emit false/null (stellar/js-stellar-sdk → isDeployableContract:false; stellar/go and stellar/soroban-examples → codeVerified:null), so true here is a misclassification, not a looser field semantic. (6) It's a class, not a one-off: stellar/stellar-horizon (GitHub languages: 3.35MB Go, 5.4KB Rust) also gets isDeployableContract:true + stellarProof:"cargo-sdk" + sorobanSdkVersion:null via /api/repos/search?q=horizon — vendored/incidental Rust with soroban crates in the dependency tree is being read as a deployable contract. Root-cause hypothesis for the fixer: the cargo scan keys on soroban crates appearing anywhere in the manifest/lock (including soroban-env-host and transitive deps) instead of requiring a workspace member with crate-type cdylib + a direct soroban-sdk dependency.

verdict.evidence:
CLAIM REPRODUCES EXACTLY, and re-verification found stronger in-corpus proof than the claim itself offered.

1) Primary probe re-run (https://stellarlight.xyz/api/research?q=transaction%20fees%20and%20resource%20limits%20on%20soroban&limit=5): rank 1 = simulateTransaction Deep-Dive "Fees and Resource Usage" (good, high 0.84). Ranks 2-4 = Hubble gold data-dictionary "Column Details" schema tables exactly as claimed: "Daily Fee Stats Agg" 0.77 high, "Ledger Fee Stats Agg" 0.76 high, "Hourly Fee Agg Account" 0.75 high. Confirmed verbatim.

2) The expectation is FAIR and provable from in-corpus data — this is the key adversarial check. The claim anchored its expectation on /docs/learn/fundamentals (not in corpus), which alone would make it a coverage gap, not a recall miss. But the corpus CONTAINS the on-topic conceptual doc: "Fees & Metering" (https://developers.stellar.org/docs/build/guides/fees), whose own prose reads "Fees and metering in Soroban smart contracts work differently than the fees for 'regular' Stellar transactions" — a direct match for the query by the record's own data. Re-running the probe at limit=15 shows it ranks #7 (0.73 medium), BELOW the three off-topic warehouse column dictionaries (0.75-0.77 'high'). Record's own data implies the query, record absent from top-5 → RECALL-MISS is provable purely in-corpus, with WRONG-TOP flavor (DDL schema tables outranking the on-topic conceptual doc, and labeled higher confidence).

3) Corpus-coverage assertion corroborated: across 9 distinct fundamentals-shaped queries (~60 result rows: the two claim probes plus 'fees resource limits metering', 'stellar consensus protocol explained', 'operations and transactions overview', 'minimum balance base reserve account', 'anatomy of a soroban smart contract', 'what are lumens XLM', 'soroban resource limits'), ZERO results under /docs/learn/**. Corpus visibly spans /docs/build/**, /docs/data/**, stellar.org blog, SEPs, SCF handbook, stellarsecurityportal, SCP whitepaper, /meetings — learn tree absent.

4) Companion probe re-run confirms as claimed: all top-5 are Hubble bronze/silver schema pages (History Ledgers, Restored Key, History Operations, Trustlines Snapshot, Accounts — 0.67-0.70). Caveat: since no learn content exists in-corpus, the bronze History Ledgers chunk (which itself defines ledger, lumens, stroop, base reserve in its column notes) is arguably the best AVAILABLE answer and confidence there is honestly 'medium' — the companion probe alone evidences the coverage gap, not a retrieval defect. The primary probe is where the graded RECALL-MISS stands.

Two incidental notes surfaced during verification (not part of this claim): (a) the in-corpus Fees & Metering chunk is a thin category-index chunk containing breadcrumb nav text ('- How-To Guides - Fees & Metering'), likely depressing its rank — chunk-hygiene relevant; (b) query 'soroban resource limits' returns meeting-author nav pages ('Tyler van der Hoeven' /meetings/authors/kalepail, 'Carsten Jacobsen', date page /meetings/2024/12/12) in top-5 — JUNK-ROW class, separate finding.

Verdict: RECALL-MISS label CORRECT, re-anchored to the in-corpus expected record /docs/build/guides/fees (absent from top-5) rather than the out-of-corpus /docs/learn/fundamentals page; the /docs/learn/** ingestion gap is a real, corroborated corpus-coverage finding attached to the same cell.

verdict.evidence:
CONFIRMED — both probes reproduce byte-for-byte and the expectation is fair.

Probe 1 (re-run): https://stellarlight.xyz/api/research?q=how%20to%20set%20up%20a%20soroban%20project&limit=5
- Rank 2 = "Develop contract with frontend templates" (soroban-contract-init-template), chunkIndex 5, section="📄️Work with contract specs in Java, Python, and PHP". Content is literal Docusaurus chrome: "Edit this pageLast updated on Jun 16, 2025 by Bri WyldePreviousInitialize a dapp using scriptsNextImplement state archival in dapps" followed by a TOC bullet list ("1. Initialize an NPM project / 2. git init / …"). The section label is a 📄️ nav-card heading for a DIFFERENT guide (Java/Python/PHP contract specs), so the chunk also misattributes its content.
- Rank 3 = "Initialize a dapp using scripts", chunkIndex 9, same mislabeled section, content = "Edit this pageLast updated on Dec 1, 2025 by Carsten JacobsenPreviousComprehensive frontend guide for Stellar dappsNextDevelop contract with frontend templates" + TOC bullets. Identical class.
- (Not in the claim, but rank 5 is also chrome: "Want to learn more?… Loading form... ### EnglishEspañol" from an sdf-blog page.)

Probe 2 (re-run): https://stellarlight.xyz/api/research?q=restore%20archived%20contract%20data%20state%20archival%20ttl&limit=5 — rank 5 = "Extend a persistent entry and contract using the JavaScript SDK", chunkIndex 6, section='📄️ Test TTL extension logic in smart contracts', content = "Edit this pageLast updated on Apr 9, 2025 by Levai!PreviousCreate a restoration footprint…NextRestore a contract…" + TOC. Exactly as claimed.

Fairness checks (refutation attempts, all failed):
1. Expected records exist and are substantive: the same two documents have real content chunks in corpus — soroban-contract-init-template chunk 2 ("Make your own template… When you run stellar contract init…") ranks #1 on q=soroban contract init template initialize npm project frontend template, and dapps/initialization chunk 4 ("Automating Initialization with Scripts… script should automate setting up the network, generating token…") ranks #1 on q=automating token initialization mint deploy script dapp. So the corpus HAS substantive setup chunks for both pages.
2. The API meta declares "best chunk per document" — meaning the footer chunk was selected as each document's representative chunk for the natural query, DISPLACING the substantive chunk of the same doc. This is worse than a stray junk row: the right doc surfaces with junk as its face.
3. "The TOC is informative" defense: rank 2's TOC does enumerate setup steps, but the chunk is still footer chrome + a section field naming an unrelated guide — an agent citing it would misattribute content; the junk classification is query-independent (content itself is chrome).
4. Tolerance is irrelevant for JUNK-ROW — the rows are served in top-5 either way.

Label JUNK-ROW is correct (secondary flavor: mislabeled section field, but the primary defect is nav/footer/TOC chunks surviving chunk hygiene — the nav-card filter misses Docusaurus footer+TOC blocks and 📄️ nav-card headings absorbed as sections).

verdict.evidence: CONFIRMED, and understated. (1) Original probe reproduces exactly: /api/research?q=run%20local%20network%20quickstart%20docker%20container&limit=5 returns ranks 2-3 as two SEPARATE documents (ids 6a15161dd5ac3338bbd5010e and 6a33c46925b81c4653965930) at URLs .../meetings/authors/carstenjacobsen/authors/2 and .../page/2 with byte-identical content ("This week we'll dive into one of the most overlooked tools - Quickstart...") and identical scores 0.6870191693305969 — the per-document best-chunk dedup can't collapse them because they're distinct corpus documents. The page/3 instance at rank 5 of the hello-world probe also reproduces. (2) The expectation is fair: the canonical page developers.stellar.org/meetings/2025/04/17 EXISTS in the corpus — probe q=Quickstart%20local%20Stellar%20network%20node%20install%20deploy%20hello%20world%20dapp&limit=10 returns it at rank 5 (0.7765) while the dupe archive pair outranks it at ranks 1-2 (both 0.8003). So this is not "archive pages standing in for missing canonicals" — the canonical is present and being beaten by two copies of its own teaser text. (3) Systematic, not isolated: q=carsten%20jacobsen%20developer%20advocate&limit=10 fills the ENTIRE top 5 with archive documents of one author (base, /authors/2, /authors/3 at 0.8614; /page/2, /page/3 at 0.8608); q=developer%20meeting%20april%202025 has 5 of 10 slots as carstenjacobsen archive pages. (4) Aggravating fact the claim missed: the /authors/N URL variant is a crawler artifact — curl -L .../authors/carstenjacobsen/authors/2 returns HTTP 404 on the live docs site (while /page/2 is 200). The corpus contains documents at URLs that do not exist, so an agent citing them hands users dead links. JUNK-ROW is the right label (archive/pagination boilerplate indexed as documents, with a dupe-pair sub-symptom); the /authors/N 404s add a DATA-WRONG flavor (corpus URL field points at a nonexistent page).

confirmed.area: freshness metadata — publishedAt null on dev-docs
confirmed.expected: dev-docs records should carry publishedAt extracted from the in-page 'Last updated on <date>' stamp their own content contains (e.g. deploy-to-testnet chunk literally says 'Last updated on Feb 24, 2026 by Copilot'); scoreModel says confidence = 0.65·relevance + 0.15·freshness + 0.20·authority
confirmed.observed: Every dev-docs row across all 23 probes has publishedAt:null, ageDays:null, freshness pinned to 1.0. The freshness component is inert on the largest source (staleness in dev-docs would be undetectable/unpenalized) while dated sources (sdf-blog) are the only ones the freshness term can demote — the date is sitting in the chunk prose but not the structured field.

verdict.evidence: CONFIRMED — reproduced and extended. (1) Original probe re-run (https://stellarlight.xyz/api/research?q=deploy%20a%20smart%20contract%20to%20testnet&limit=5): 3/3 rows are dev-docs with "publishedAt":null, "ageDays":null, "freshness":1, and the deploy-to-testnet row's own content literally contains the date: "Edit this pageLast updated on Feb 24, 2026 by CopilotPrevious1. Hello World...". The meta.scoreModel note is verbatim: "confidence.score = 0.65·relevance + 0.15·freshness + 0.20·authority" — freshness is a declared 15% component. (2) Systemic, not a partial-ingest gap: 26 more dev-docs rows across 5 additional queries (source=dev-docs: stellar-asset-contract/token, run a validator node, fee bump transaction, anchor SEP-24, soroban auth) — 0/26 non-null publishedAt, freshness uniformly {1}; 3/10 chunks on one query carry the in-prose "Last updated on" Docusaurus stamp (it's a page-footer chunk, i.e. document-level metadata — exactly where publishedAt lives). (3) The asymmetry is real: sdf-blog rows carry publishedAt and are demoted by the freshness term (Protocol 24 Upgrade Guide 2025-10-20 → freshness 0.71 @263d; Protocol 21 → 0.36 @793d; Protocol 19 → 0.14 @1526d), while null-dated dev-docs get the MAXIMUM freshness (1.0). So a genuinely stale docs page is unpenalizable, and the freshness term functions only as a demotion on dated sources relative to undated ones. (4) Fairness check passed: the expectation derives from the API's own declared scoring contract plus the record's own prose containing the date — not from taste; null→1.0 is not defensible "correct-but-different" behavior because it inverts the term's stated purpose (unknown age scores strictly better than any known age). Minor caveat, not weakening the claim: freshness appears source-aware — an audit row dated 2025-09-12 (~300d) also shows freshness 1, so "dated sources are the only ones demoted" is slightly overbroad (sdf-blog demoted, audit apparently not); the dev-docs defect stands regardless.
verdict.correctedMode: DATA-WRONG (label correct — structured field publishedAt missing vs the record's own in-prose 'Last updated on' stamp)

verdict.evidence:
CONFIRMED — all 3 claimed probes reproduce today (2026-07-09) plus a fresh 4th probe I chose independently, so it is systemic, not query-specific. Attempted refutations all failed.

(1) Primary probe /api/research?q=muxed%20accounts%20M%20addresses%20multiplexed&limit=5: meta.scoreModel.note still declares "Results are returned in confidence order, best chunk per document", yet ranks 1/4/5 are three chunks (chunkIndex 6/2/8) of the SAME dev-docs page (pooled-accounts-muxed-accounts-memos) at conf 0.85/0.81/0.79, and confidence order is 0.85, 0.64, 0.59, 0.81, 0.79 — non-monotonic. (2) Clawback probe (?q=clawback%20asset%20issuer%20revoke&limit=8): Clawbacks chunk0 at #1 conf 0.68 AND chunk1 at #8 conf 0.64, seated below two conf-0.39 rows. (3) Sponsored-reserves probe: confidence sequence 0.75, 0.69, 0.66, 0.49, 0.73 with ranks 1 and 5 both the same Sponsored-reserves doc (chunks 0 and 2).

Refutation attempt A — "maybe it's sorted by raw vector score, and only the note is stale": FAILS; raw scores are also non-monotonic (muxed: 0.7857, 0.7758, 0.7484, 0.7652, 0.7591; clawback rank 8 raw 0.6907 > ranks 3-7). Refutation attempt B — "expectation is auditor taste": FAILS; the expectation is the endpoint's own served contract in meta.scoreModel.note, i.e. ground truth from the response itself. Refutation attempt C — "harmless ordering nit within tolerance": FAILS; 3 of 5 top-K slots go to one document, and at limit=10 the same query surfaces SEP-23 (github.com/stellar/stellar-protocol/.../sep-0023.md, conf 0.70 — the actual spec defining M-addresses) which is crowded out of top-5 by the duplicate chunks, so real recall is lost.

New diagnostic detail for the fixer: the pattern is consistent with dedupe-then-backfill — distinct documents appear first in proper confidence order, then extra chunks of already-listed documents are APPENDED at the tail to fill the limit (muxed: dupes at ranks 4-5; clawback limit=8: dupe at rank 8; sponsored limit=5: dupe at rank 5; fresh probe ?q=path%20payment%20strict%20send%20receive&limit=5: list-strict-receive-payment-paths chunks 1 AND 2 at ranks 3 and 5, conf 0.77/0.75, non-monotonic vs rank 4's 0.74). Also note fetch composition varies with limit (limit=5 vs limit=10 return different candidate pools), suggesting the vector-search candidate count is derived from limit. Side observation (separate issue, not this claim): at limit=10 the muxed query returns identical meeting content under two URLs (developers.stellar.org/meetings/authors/carstenjacobsen/authors/2 and .../page/2), a same-content-two-URL dupe.

confirmed.expected: SEP-8 Regulated Assets (sep-0008.md, in-corpus, conf 0.59, chunk 6 discusses clawback-style regulated-asset control) appears at #2 when limit=8, so correct per-doc top-5 = Clawbacks, SEP-8, then 3 blog posts
confirmed.observed: At limit=5 the authoritative sep row is ABSENT from top-5; its slot is taken by a duplicate second chunk of the rank-1 Clawbacks doc (conf 0.64). Result set is not a prefix of the limit=8 set — requested K changes membership, not just truncation. Same root cause as the dedupe finding; reproducible/deterministic across re-runs

verdict.evidence: CONFIRMED, deterministic across re-runs. Probe: /api/research?q=clawback%20asset%20issuer%20revoke at limits 5/8/10/12/15/20. (1) RECALL-MISS at limit=5: results = [Clawbacks chunk0 conf 0.68, Protocol-17 blog conf 0.48, Protecting-Value blog conf 0.45, Using-P17 blog conf 0.45, Clawbacks chunk1 conf 0.65]. The sep-0008 "Regulated Assets" row (chunk 6, conf 0.59 — the system's OWN second-highest confidence for this query) is absent; slot 5 is instead a duplicate second chunk of the rank-1 doc, directly violating the response's own meta contract "confidence order, best chunk per document". (2) Membership-not-truncation confirmed: limit=5 set is not a prefix of limit=8 (5th member: Clawbacks-chunk1 vs Asset-Sandbox blog), and limit=8 is radically different from limit=10+ — at limit=8 the server serves rows it scores 0.39–0.48 while omitting rows it scores 0.51–0.59 (sep-0008 0.59, Asset Address sep 0.55, Verseprop audit 0.54, etc.) that appear at limit≥10. (3) One detail correction to the claim: sep-0008 lands at #2 at limit=10/12/15/20, NOT at limit=8 as claimed — at limit=8 it is still absent, so the defect is slightly worse than stated; use &limit=10 as the comparator in the repro. (4) Corroborating ordering bug: the duplicate Clawbacks chunk (conf 0.64) is appended at position 8 of the limit=8 response AFTER conf-0.39 rows, i.e. not in confidence order — consistent with a limit-scaled candidate pool being deduped/padded post-hoc (same root cause as the dedupe finding). Expectation fairness: grading is grounded in the endpoint's own confidence model and stated ordering contract, not taste; sep-0008 chunk 6 (regulated-asset revocation/approval) is squarely on-topic for "clawback asset issuer revoke".
verdict.correctedMode: RECALL-MISS (label correct; fix probe comparator from &limit=8 to &limit=10 — sep-0008 first appears at #2 at limit=10)

verdict.evidence: Re-ran the probe (https://stellarlight.xyz/api/research?q=Core%20Advancement%20Proposal%20CAP%20protocol%20change%20stellar-core&limit=8): reproduces exactly. All 8 rows are developers.stellar.org/meetings pages; rows 2-4 are the SAME author archive under three URL variants — /meetings/authors/carstenjacobsen (conf 0.78 'high'), /meetings/authors/carstenjacobsen/authors/2 (0.78 'high'), /meetings/authors/carstenjacobsen/page/2 (0.78 'high') — with rows 3 and 4 byte-identical in chunk text AND raw score (0.7515345811843872), and that same chunk duplicated a third time at the canonical /meetings/2025/02/20 (row 5, 0.77 'high'). The class is broader than claimed: a probe q='protocol upgrade governance validators vote'&source=dev-docs returned 5/6 author-archive rows including a FOURTH variant (/meetings/authors/carstenjacobsen/page/3, conf 0.56) and the bare author archive at conf 0.99. Crowding-out is provable in-corpus: source=sdf-blog holds substantive protocol docs ('Announcing Protocol 23', 'Parallelizing Stellar Core', 'Road to 5000 TPS', stellar-core release posts) yet zero non-/meetings docs make the unfiltered top-8. TWO QUALIFICATIONS: (1) the '8/8 junk' framing overstates — the date-titled canonical pages (2024-03-14, 2024-08-29, 2025-09-25) are substantive protocol-meeting notes whose content IS CAP deliberations ('CAP Core Team deliberated over the latest proposals... to advance stellar-core', CAP-51/53–56), i.e. legitimate on-topic results for this query, not junk; the defect is specifically the 3 archive/pagination duplicate URLs indexed as separate documents. Fix target: exclude/canonicalize /meetings/authors/* and */page/N (+ the malformed nested /authors/N) URLs at ingest.
verdict.correctedMode: JUNK-ROW (scoped: the 3 author-archive/pagination duplicate URLs in top-8, not all 8 rows — date-titled canonical meeting notes are substantive on-topic docs)

verdict.evidence:
CONFIRMED — reproduced all three instances and identified the mechanism; the DATA-WRONG label is correct.

1) Probe re-run (https://stellarlight.xyz/api/research?q=stellar.toml%20SEP-1%20service%20discovery%20metadata&limit=5) returns, in one response: rank1 sep-0001 title='Sample stellar.toml' section='Specification'; rank2 sep-0020 title='Simple Summary' section='Stellar.toml metadata'; rank4 sep-0046 title='Contract Meta'; rank5 sep-0019 title='Bootstrapping Multisig Transaction Submission'. So the schema's own intent (title=doc title, section=chunk heading) is demonstrated by rows 4-5 in the same payload, and rows 1-2 violate it.

2) Ground truth from the records' own source docs (raw.githubusercontent.com/stellar/stellar-protocol/master/ecosystem/): sep-0001.md preamble line 5 = 'Title: Stellar Info File'; 'Sample stellar.toml' is an H1 at line 257 (an example section near the end — it doesn't even belong to the 'Specification' chunk being served, which is lines 33-222, so the title is incoherent under ANY reading). sep-0020.md line 3 = 'Title: Self-verification of validator nodes'; 'Simple Summary' is the boilerplate first H1 at line 10 — meaningless as a row label.

3) sep-0025 also reproduced: https://stellarlight.xyz/api/research?q=SEP-25%20trustlines%20zero%20balance%20accounts&limit=6 returns rank6 sep-0025 title='Providers Transaction:' section='Preamble'. Canonical title is 'Title: New account deposit', and '# Providers Transaction:' at line 58 is a comment line INSIDE a fenced code block — not even a markdown heading.

4) Mechanism (explains inconsistency exactly): the title extractor takes the first '# ' line in the file; SEP-19/24 contain no H1s (they use '##' throughout) so extraction falls back to the correct preamble Title, while older-style SEPs using H1 section headings (sep-0020) or containing '#'-comment lines in code fences (sep-0001 line 257 context, sep-0025) get a section heading or code comment as the document title. Not correct-but-different behavior: the same response mixes both semantics, and the fair fix (preamble 'Title:' field, present in every SEP) is unambiguous. Also cross-checked the claim's 'as they do elsewhere' premise: q='hosted deposit and withdrawal anchor interactive' returns sep-0024 row titled 'Hosted Deposit and Withdrawal' (correct doc title). Minor nit only: SEP-1's canonical title is exactly 'Stellar Info File' (the claim's 'Stellar Info File / stellar.toml' gloss is informal but substantively right).

verdict.evidence: CONFIRMED — reproduced and the expectation is fair. (1) Ground truth verified: /api/research?q=incident&source=incident returns exactly ONE incident record in the corpus: "Blend Protocol — oracle-manipulation incident (May 2026, attempted & contained)", severity=high, publishedAt 2026-05-22, content: "...target of an attempted ~$10 million oracle-manipulation attack..." — its own data (source=incident, 'attack', 'incident') canonically implies the query. (3) Second phrasing q=what%20hacks%20have%20happened%20on%20Stellar: incident at rank 11, just outside top-10 (borderline fail on its own, but consistent). (4) Ambiguity defense ('hacks'=hackathon projects) fails: the engine itself read the query as security — 7-8 of top-10 are threat-model/audit/security docs — yet omitted the corpus's only actual incident while serving a pizza-restaurant STRIDE template example. (5) Not a dead record: it retrieves fine on synonyms — "stellar exploits" #2, "oracle manipulation attack" #3, "blend protocol incident" #2, "has stellar ever been hacked" #4. So this is a phrasing-specific embedding gap on the plural noun "hacks" (vector mode, voyage-3): the single most canonical short agent query for the incident lane misses the record entirely. RECALL-MISS label is correct.

verdict.evidence:
Re-ran all three probes live (2026-07-09). CONFIRMED, byte-for-byte.

PRIMARY probe /api/research?q=Blend%20hack%20May%202026&limit=10: ranks 1-3 are the IDENTICAL chunk — content strings byte-equal (verified c[0]==c[1]==c[2] → True) with identical vector score 0.7194640636444092 to 16 decimals — served from 3 URLs: developers.stellar.org/meetings/2025/02/27 (canonical post, chunkIndex 1), /meetings/authors/carstenjacobsen/authors/2 (chunkIndex 13), /meetings/authors/carstenjacobsen/page/2 (chunkIndex 13). The latter two are author-pagination index pages ingested as separate documents, titled 'Carsten Jacobsen'. Rank 10 is a FOURTH carstenjacobsen author-index doc — 4 of 10 slots are crawler artifacts of one meeting note. Rank 7 'Archive' (developers.stellar.org/meetings/archive) is a pure nav row: content is just a bare date list ('### 2026 - January 22 - 2026-01-22 - January 29 …'). The on-topic incident record ('Blend Protocol — oracle-manipulation incident (May 2026, attempted & contained)', whose title/content literally contain Blend + May 2026 + attack) sits at #6 — outside the top-5 niche tolerance, demoted specifically by the triplicate. Expectation is fair: the record's own title implies this query, and the API's own scoreModel note promises 'best chunk per document' — a guarantee hollowed out by duplicate documents in the corpus.

SECONDARY probes reproduce exactly as claimed: 'is Blend safe to build on' → same triplicate chunk at #3-5 (incident correctly #1 here, so this probe shows slot-waste not recall failure); 'Reflector oracle audit' → author-pagination junk ('Part 1'/'Carsten Jacobsen', /meetings/authors/carstenjacobsen/authors/3) at #9-10.

Not explainable as correct-but-different behavior: the three top rows are not distinct documents in any user-meaningful sense — same prose, same score, crawler URL variants. One minor caveat: the '2024-06-20' row at #5 is date-TITLED but its content is genuinely Blend-related (Meru×Blend integration meeting note), so it is a title-hygiene defect more than a pure junk row; this does not weaken the core finding. JUNK-ROW label is correct (pagination-index dupes + nav 'Archive' row), with a knock-on top-5 recall demotion of the incident record on the primary probe.

verdict.evidence: Re-ran both probes; both reproduce deterministically (probe 2 run twice, identical). Probe 1 (q=lending+oracle+dex+token+vault&source=audit&limit=50, clamped to 25): meta note says verbatim "Results are returned in confidence order, best chunk per document", yet report/75 appears in 9 of 25 rows (ranks 1,16,17,18,20,21,22,23,24; chunks {0,2,4,5,6,7,8,9,10} — exact match to claim) and report/18 in 3 rows; confidence order breaks at rank 16 (ranks 1-15 descend 0.70→0.53, rank 16 jumps to 0.68 — dedup-then-fill pattern: first 15 deduped, then duplicate chunks appended as padding). Probe 2 (q=has Soroban Governor been audited&limit=10): report/25 at rank 1 (chunk 2, conf 0.76) AND rank 10 (chunk 0, conf 0.70); rank 10's 0.70 > ranks 6-9 (0.59/0.58/0.57/0.52) — both meta-contract clauses violated in one response, exactly as claimed. Minor nuance: "mostly boilerplate" slightly overstates — chunk 0 is true boilerplate (title page/TOC: "# Security # Audit # Report ... # TableofContents"), but chunks 4-10 are substantive protocol text under '## Methodology' headers; the defect is duplicate-doc rows + broken confidence order violating the meta contract, which JUNK-ROW (redundant rows occupying top-K slots) still best captures within the given taxonomy.

verdict.evidence:
Probe re-run reproduces exactly: /api/research?q=Defindex%20vault%20audit&source=audit&limit=5 returns rank-1 record (id 6a1516ba975750ee958a3b74) with title "Soroswap Aggregator — OtterSec (Defindex)", protocol="Soroswap Aggregator", url stellarsecurityportal.com/report/43. Ground truth from the upstream portal API (stellarsecurityportal.com/api/v1/reports/43): name="Defindex", body opens "# Defindex

Security Assessment
March 18th, 2025 — Prepared by OtterSec", scope = github.com/paltalabs/defindex (factory/vault/strategies — a yield-vault product, not an aggregator), all finding IDs are OS-DIX-*, page headers read "Defindex Audit", and the critical OS-DIX-ADV-00 "bRate Precision Manipulation to Gain Unfair Profit" is present — the exact finding the claim says gets misattributed. Double-count confirmed: ?q=Soroswap%20Aggregator%20audit&source=audit returns BOTH report/29 (Runtime Verification, genuinely Soroswap Aggregator) and report/43 (OtterSec, actually DeFindex) with protocol="Soroswap Aggregator", so a protocol-faceting agent sees 2 Soroswap Aggregator audits and 0 DeFindex audits. IMPORTANT NUANCE for the fix: the error is INHERITED FROM UPSTREAM — the portal itself files report 43 under protocolId=27/protocolName="Soroswap Aggregator" and its protocol registry contains no DeFindex entity at all; our ingest mirrored it faithfully. That doesn't refute DATA-WRONG (the field is false vs the record's own prose/reality), but the remediation is an ingest-side override preferring the report's own name field ("Defindex", already captured in our title parenthetical) over the portal's protocolName for known-bad mappings. Label check: DATA-WRONG is correct — retrieval ranks the record #1 for the DeFindex query, so only the structured field is wrong (not RECALL-MISS/WRONG-TOP).

verdict.evidence: Re-ran all four probes against live API 2026-07-09; every leg reproduces. (1) /api/research?q=Curve%20Finance%20vault%20exploit&limit=10 → 10 Stellar audits (HiYield, Soroswap Aggregator, PhoenixDeFiHub, Blend V2, Huma, xycloans, comet, zkCross, Alula), confidence 0.60-0.63 ALL labeled "medium", relevance 0.41-0.46; no returned chunk mentions Curve (verified top chunk = HiYield credit-vault audit prose) — corpus provably has no Curve content, yet labels read as an answer. (2) ?q=oracle%20exploit&source=incident → single result "Blend Protocol — oracle-manipulation incident (May 2026)", own prose literally "oracle-manipulation exploit", served at confidence.score 0.4 label "low" relevance 0.14 (raw vector score 0.5906) — exact match to claim. (3) ?q=is%20Spiko%20safe → correct "Spiko — Halborn" audit first at 0.54, below every irrelevant Curve result. (4) ?q=oracle%20exploit&limit=20 unfiltered → all 20 results exactly 0.4 "low" (relevance 0.11-0.17 vs claimed 0.14-0.17, minor run-to-run drift), incident ranks #10 below 9 generic audits as claimed; ordering within the floored set shuffles between calls, confirming the 0.40 floor erases rank discrimination. Mechanism check refutes correct-but-different: off-topic Curve top hit raw cosine 0.688→relevance 0.46→medium vs on-topic oracle top hit raw 0.600→relevance 0.17→low — a 0.09 raw gap (query-length/vocabulary artifact) is amplified into a 0.29 relevance gap and a label inversion by the research-confidence-1 transform (0.65·relevance + 0.15·freshness + 0.20·authority, relevance-floored per the API's own scoreModel note). Expectation is fair: the endpoint publishes the confidence field expressly for answer-quality calibration, and it is inverted against ground truth provable from the records' own prose. Mode EMPTY-DISHONEST (confidence-calibration) is apt — a no-answer-in-corpus query is served as a medium-confidence answer.

confirmed.expected: The single source=incident record describes an attempted oracle-manipulation attack/theft — i.e. a hack. A user filtering incidents for 'hack' should get it (vector mode matches it fine: q=oracle%20exploit&source=incident returns it).
confirmed.observed: mode silently flips from vector to 'keyword' (model:null) and returns 0 results with no explanation — reads as 'no hack incidents on Stellar', which the corpus's own incident record contradicts. Same silent keyword flip on q=incident&source=incident (which happens to keyword-match).

verdict.evidence: CONFIRMED — reproduced deterministically and root-caused in code. (1) Probe re-run: /api/research?q=hack&source=incident&limit=5 → {"mode":"keyword","model":null,"counts":{"returned":0},"results":[]} — reproduced across repeats and variants (q=hacks, q=hacked also keyword/0). (2) The expectation is fair: source=incident contains exactly ONE record, "Blend Protocol — oracle-manipulation incident (May 2026, attempted & contained)", whose own prose says "the target of an attempted ~$10 million oracle-manipulation attack" — a hack by any plain reading; top-5 against a 1-record source is maximally tolerant. (3) Vector mode DOES retrieve it: q=exploit&source=incident and q=oracle%20exploit&source=incident both return {"mode":"vector","model":"voyage-3","returned":1} with that record — so the semantic lane works when it runs; the miss is not a relevance judgment. (4) Root cause (src/app/api/research/route.ts, lines 153-198 & 228-238): $vectorSearch over-fetches only max(limit*4, 24) GLOBAL candidates with the source filter applied as a post-pipeline $match; if the wanted source's chunks miss the global top-24 for the query vector, docs.length===0 triggers a thrown error and silent keyword fallback. Keyword `contains` then finds nothing because the literal string "hack" never appears in the record ("attack"/"theft" do — q=attack and q=theft keyword-match it, returned:1). (5) Also confirmed the claim's secondary observation: q=incident&source=incident flips to keyword too (happens to match, returned:1) — the flip is systemic for narrow sources, not query-specific. (6) Not correct-but-different behavior: meta discloses only the bare "mode":"keyword","model":null with zero explanation that vector was attempted and post-filtering emptied the pool; a consumer (human or Raven) reads 0 results as "no hack incidents on Stellar", which the corpus's own record contradicts. Minor precision note: "silently" is a hair strong — the mode field IS present in meta — but there is no fallback explanation or signpost, so the EMPTY-DISHONEST label stands.
verdict.correctedMode: EMPTY-DISHONEST (label correct; deterministic silent vector→keyword degrade caused by post-$match source filtering over a 24-doc global vector pool)

verdict.evidence: Re-ran both probes 2026-07-09T18:03Z, both reproduce exactly. Probe 1 (q=what is the latest stellar protocol version&limit=5): top-3 are undated SEP preamble chunks — #1 'Sample stellar.toml' SEP-0001 (conf 0.87), #2 'Federation protocol' SEP-0002 updated 2019 (conf 0.86), #3 'Compliance protocol' SEP-0003 with literal `Status: Abandoned` (conf 0.85); #4 = P26 Yardstick guide (title broken to 'Stellar'), #5 = Protocol 21 (2024); P27 Zipper absent from top-5. Probe 2 (q=recent stellar protocol changes&limit=10): positions 1-7 all SEP chunks, P26 announcement at #9, P27 absent from top-10. Fairness verified: the expected record IS in-corpus — q=protocol 27 zipper upgrade returns the P27 Zipper Upgrade Guide #1 (sdf-blog, publishedAt 2026-06-04, url .../stellar-zipper-protocol-27-upgrade-guide) but with conf only 0.66, i.e. lower than the Abandoned-SEP score on the news query. Root-cause mechanism confirmed from the API's own confidence payload: undated SEP chunks get `"freshness":1,"ageDays":null` (null publishedAt treated as perfectly fresh) plus `"authority":1` (stellar-protocol repo), vs sdf-blog `"authority":0.7` with dated freshness decay (P21: freshness 0.36) — so on any 'latest/recent' query, undated 2017-2019 boilerplate structurally beats the actual current answer; SEP preambles also lexically contain 'Version: X'/'Updated:' feeding relevance. Not correct-but-different behavior: an Abandoned 2019 SEP preamble labeled 'high' confidence at #3 is not a valid answer to 'latest protocol version' when the literal answer (P27, 2026-06-04) exists in-corpus and misses both top-5 and top-10 tolerances. WRONG-TOP label is correct (secondary JUNK-ROW flavor: the winning rows are preamble boilerplate chunks; and null-dated⇒freshness=1 is the fixable scoring defect).

verdict.evidence: CONFIRMED, and stronger than claimed. (1) Primary probe /api/research?q=stellar%20Q3%202026%20update&limit=5 re-run: #1 '2025-11-06' (date-as-title meeting stub, ends 'Newer post2026-01-22Older post2025-10-30' nav boilerplate), #2 '2025-03-06' (same class), #4 'Carsten Jacobsen' (author-archive page /meetings/authors/carstenjacobsen) — 3 of top-4 junk (claim said 4/4; #3 was a legit Protocol 26 Yardstick chunk on my run, minor overstatement). Killer detail: #5 IS the correct answer — sdf-blog 'Q3 2025: Scaling what works' quarterly report, publishedAt 2025-11-03, with the HIGHEST raw vector score in the set (0.7546 vs junk's 0.7215) — it ranks last because all junk rows have publishedAt:null which the confidence formula rewards with freshness=1.0, so undated junk structurally outranks genuinely dated news. Expectation is fair: derived from the corpus's own best-scoring record. (2) Dupes confirmed byte-identical: on q=stellar%20ecosystem%20recap%20june%202026 and q=what%20is%20new%20in%20soroban%20smart%20contracts, ranks #2/#3 are 'Carsten Jacobsen' at .../carstenjacobsen/authors/3 AND .../carstenjacobsen/page/3 — identical md5 (536b8f1a...), identical chunkIndex 6/19, identical score, two separate doc IDs (6a15161e... vs 6a33c469...). Upstream check: the /authors/3 variant returns HTTP 404 on developers.stellar.org (malformed crawl URL indexed as a corpus doc); /page/3 returns 200. So the corpus serves a dead-URL duplicate in top-5. (3) Recurrence: the dupe pair also appeared in a FRESH probe I chose (q=stellar%20quarterly%20report%20Q1%202026, ranks #3/#4, plus '2025-03-06' at #2 and '2025-01-16' at #5) — pervasive, not cherry-picked. (4) On the recap probe the on-topic 'Stellar Weekly Roundup — week of Jun 19, 2026' (lumenloop, pubAt 2026-06-26) again had the highest raw score (0.7699) yet ranked #5 below the dupes. Not explainable as correct-but-different: identical duplicate rows and a 404 URL have no defensible reading, and the freshness=1.0-for-null-date mechanism provably inverts ranking against the API's own scoreModel intent. Minor corrections only: pagination index of the dupe pair varies by query (/2 vs /3 — multiple dupe pages exist, worse), and primary-probe top-4 contained one legit row.
verdict.correctedMode: JUNK-ROW (correct; compound — the junk-over-news inversion is mechanically caused by publishedAt:null → freshness=1.0 in research-confidence-1, and the author-archive dupes include a 404 crawl URL, so a DATA-WRONG/dedupe fix on null dates + URL canonicalization would resolve all probes)

confirmed.expected: publishedAt for dev-docs meeting pages should be set — the record's own prose states 'November 6, 2025 · One min read' and the URL itself is /meetings/2025/11/06
confirmed.observed: publishedAt=null on ALL dev-docs rows (every dev-docs result across 18 probes was NO-DATE). With confidence = 0.65·relevance+0.15·freshness+0.20·authority, undated chunks are immune to freshness scoring, which is why undated junk beats dated current news throughout this cell
confirmed.area: dev-docs publishedAt missing (root cause of freshness failures)

verdict.evidence: REPRODUCED. Probe /api/research?q=stellar%20Q3%202026%20update&limit=5: all 4 dev-docs rows have "publishedAt":null while their own content states the date — e.g. id 6a15161bd5ac3338bbd5008c, url https://developers.stellar.org/meetings/2025/11/06, content begins "# 2025-11-06 ... November 6, 2025 · One min readCarsten Jacobsen". Date is derivable from both prose and URL path. Widened: /api/research?source=dev-docs&q=meeting&limit=25 → 25/25 publishedAt=null; 5 more queries (soroban meeting, protocol upgrade, developer walkthrough, passkeys) → every dev-docs row null. Not a schema absence: sdf-blog rows in the same responses carry publishedAt (e.g. 2025-11-03T00:00:00.000Z, freshness 0.63, ageDays 364/248), proving per-source population. Root cause confirmed in repo: scripts/ingest-developers-docs.ts contains zero occurrences of publishedAt. MECHANISM CORRECTION: the claim's "undated chunks are immune to freshness" is misattributed — src/lib/confidence.ts puts dev-docs in the EVERGREEN set (`if (EVERGREEN.has(source)) return { freshness: 1, ageDays: null }`), so freshness=1 unconditionally; the null-date branch (freshness 0.6 "neutral") is never reached for dev-docs, and populating publishedAt alone would not change ranking. Observable harm stands and is worse than "immunity": in the original probe the actual Q3 quarterly report (sdf-blog, raw score 0.7546 = highest relevance in the set) ranked 5th below four meeting/author-archive chunks (raw 0.716–0.721) because evergreen freshness=1 + authority 0.95 beat its 0.73 freshness + 0.70 authority. Fix requires BOTH setting publishedAt from prose/URL for dated /meetings/ pages AND excluding those dated pages from the evergreen classification. Scope caveat: only meeting/dated pages are provably DATA-WRONG; /docs/ reference pages have no publish date in their own content, so null there is defensible.
verdict.correctedMode: DATA-WRONG (label correct; causal note corrected — freshness immunity comes from the EVERGREEN source classification in src/lib/confidence.ts, not from the null publishedAt itself)

verdict.evidence: Re-ran the exact probe https://stellarlight.xyz/api/research?q=latest%20stellar%20quarterly%20report&limit=5 — top-5 returned: #1 "Messari State of Stellar Q1 2026" (dev-docs, conf 0.67), #2 Weekly Roundup Jun 5 2026, #3 USST article, #4 "Stellar | Q1 2025 Quarterly Report: The impact is HERE!" (sdf-blog, publishedAt 2025-05-08, conf 0.6), #5 Weekly Roundup Jun 26 2026. The current edition "Stellar | Q1 2026: Execution at network scale" (sdf-blog, publishedAt 2026-05-07) is ABSENT from top-5. In-corpus confirmed: on q=Q1%202026%20quarterly%20report%20execution%20at%20network%20scale it ranks #1 with conf 0.8 (relevance 0.8, freshness 0.92, ageDays 64). With limit=20 on the original probe, the Q1 2026 report appears only at rank #19 (conf 0.57) — BELOW its superseded year-old predecessor Q1 2025 at rank #10 (conf 0.6), despite the scoreModel advertising a 0.15 freshness component. Expectation is fair: both records are editions of the same sdf-blog quarterly-report series, so "latest quarterly report" is directly implied by their own titles/source, and serving the 2025 edition while the 2026 one exists is stale-serving, not a taste call. Mechanism appears lexical (2025 title literally contains "Quarterly Report"; 2026 title is "Execution at network scale"), but that explains, not excuses, the failure. One softening nuance for the report: rank #1 IS current Q1 2026 quarterly content (Messari's State of Stellar via dev-docs meeting notes), so the claim's line "gets the 2025 edition" is only true for the SDF-report-series slice — but the STALE-SERVED core (outdated edition served where the current edition exists in-corpus and is retrievable) is confirmed.

verdict.evidence: Re-ran the exact probe (2026-07-09T18:04Z): https://stellarlight.xyz/api/research?q=stellar%20protocol%2027%20zipper&limit=5 reproduces the claim byte-for-byte. #1 = sep/SEP-0003 'Compliance protocol' Preamble chunk whose own content reads "Status: Abandoned / Created: 2017-10-30 / Updated: 2019-10-04" (confidence 0.68); #2 = the P27 doc (url .../stellar-zipper-protocol-27-upgrade-guide, publishedAt 2026-06-04) with title displayed as just "Stellar" (confidence 0.67). The mechanism is provable from the response itself: raw vector score ranks the Zipper guide FIRST (0.7299 vs 0.7043), but the confidence formula (0.65·relevance + 0.15·freshness + 0.20·authority) flips it — the undated SEP-3 gets freshness=1.0 (ageDays:null treated as fresh) and authority=1.0 (sep source), vs the Zipper guide's 0.95 freshness / 0.7 authority: 0.65·0.51+0.15·1+0.20·1=0.68 > 0.65·0.60+0.15·0.95+0.20·0.70=0.67. Expectation fairness checks out: this is a known-item query, the Zipper guide is the only in-corpus doc matching 'Zipper, Protocol 27' (its URL slug is the query), and it is maximally current (mainnet vote July 8, 2026 — yesterday). SEP-3 is not an 'equally-valid' alternative — an Abandoned 2017 compliance SEP is unambiguously off-topic for Protocol 27, so this is WRONG-TOP, not a top-K ordering-taste quibble. Not cherry-picked: variant q='zipper upgrade' shows the same shape (#1 = off-topic SEP-49 'Upgradeable Contracts' preamble, Zipper guide again #2 titled 'Stellar'); variant q='protocol 27' is worse (top-5 = meeting-date junk pages '2025-02-27', '2025-03-27' matching the numeral; Zipper guide absent). The title defect is also real and doc-specific: sibling blog docs render full titles ('Stellar | Protocol 24 Upgrade Guide', 'Stellar | Yardstick, Stellar Protocol 26') while this doc's title collapsed to 'Stellar', so even at #2 an agent scanning titles cannot recognize the answer. Two aggravating sub-defects ride along: undated-⇒-freshness=1.0 scoring (rewards the abandoned SEP) and the truncated title extraction on the Zipper doc (DATA-WRONG flavor), but the primary WRONG-TOP label is correct as filed.

verdict.evidence: Re-ran all probes 3x each — responses are deterministic per limit, but limits contradict each other. (1) Score instability limit=5 vs limit=10 CONFIRMED: identical chunk (id …4d96e6, 'Lumen Loop: one tab', chunkIndex 4) scores conf 0.63/rel 0.57 at limit=5 but conf 0.65/rel 0.60 at limit=10; Yardstick 'Sources' chunk jumps #5→#3 and 'Meet the Stellar Ecosystem Podcast — Listen and contribute' enters at #4, pushing fresh 'Weekly Roundup Jun 26' out of the top-5 window. Relevance is normalized within the candidate set. (2) The claim's dramatic junk-leapfrog reproduces EXACTLY at limit=20 (not limit=10 today — index likely shifted since the claim was filed): https://stellarlight.xyz/api/research?q=latest%20stellar%20news&limit=20 returns #1 'Part 1' (dev-docs, undated, conf 0.57, rel 0.36), #2-3 'Carsten Jacobsen' x2 (dev-docs, undated, conf 0.57), and ranks #4-9 are ALL undated dev-docs boilerplate ('Stellar Basics', 'Extend a deployed contract's TTL', 'MPP on Stellar — Install'), while the same Lumen Loop doc is rescored to conf 0.52/rel 0.39 at #10 — matching the claim's cited 0.57-junk/0.52-Lumen-Loop numbers verbatim. Root cause visible in the returned fields: junk wins despite LOWER relevance (0.36 vs 0.39) because undated dev-docs apparently get full freshness credit + higher authority under conf = 0.65·rel + 0.15·fresh + 0.20·auth, and larger limits widen the vector candidate pool to include them at all. So an agent paging deeper gets a strictly worse, contradictory top — WRONG-TOP confirmed, compounded by JUNK-ROW (nav/author-name chunks) and arguably DATA-WRONG (missing publishedAt treated as fresh). Only correction: the reproducing limit today is 20, not 10; limit=10 vs 5 still shows the score/rank drift but with clean top rows.
verdict.correctedMode: WRONG-TOP (holds; also JUNK-ROW — undated dev-docs nav/author chunks at #1-9). Note: junk-leapfrog now reproduces at limit=20 rather than limit=10; limit=5 vs 10 still proves limit-dependent scoring on identical chunks.

verdict.evidence: Re-ran all probes against live API 2026-07-09. (1) /api/partners?type=protocol → {"counts":{"returned":0,"total":0},"partners":[]}. (2) /api/partners?type=protocol&all=1 → {"counts":{"returned":4,"total":4}} with exactly the 4 claimed slugs (blend, soroswap, aquarius, phoenix-protocol), each tagline:null, contactEmail:null, contactChannel:null, freshness.status:"fresh", trust:{"score":0.7,"label":"high"} — so all 4 fail the quality bar solely on missing tagline/contact, per the response's own note: "Default results pass a directory quality bar (tagline + contact path, non-archived); pass all=1 for the unfiltered set." (3) type=infrastructure → 1 (defindex) vs all=1 → 2 (reflector hidden, tagline null). (4) type=tooling → 1 (trustless-work) vs all=1 → 2 (stellarexpert hidden, tagline null). (5) Corroborating sweep: default unfiltered list = 26/47 partners; the protocol lane is 100% dark (0/4) and so is wallet (0/5), while both values are advertised in the response's validTypes. Refutation attempt: the emptiness is not fully silent — meta.note discloses the quality bar + all=1 escape hatch in every response, and the type filter is echoed, which softens "dishonest." But there is no per-query signal that hidden records exist (total:0 counts only the post-bar set; no hiddenByQualityBar count), so a consumer cannot distinguish "no protocol partners exist" from "4 fresh high-trust ones hidden" — the taxonomy's exact "wrong-looking 0 results" case. Expectation is fair: the records' own structured data (published, fresh, trust=high, partnerType=protocol) implies type=protocol must retrieve them. Claim CONFIRMED; note the underlying root cause is a data-completeness gap (seeded protocol/wallet partners lack tagline/contact) and the cleanest fix is either filling those fields or adding a hidden-count/note when total=0 with records below the bar.

verdict.evidence: Re-ran all probes 2026-07-09. (1) COUNTS EXACT: /api/partners?all=1&limit=100 → 47 total (45 fresh + 2 archived, meta.counts.returned=47); default /api/partners?limit=100 → 26 (all fresh, all with taglines). 19 of 45 fresh have tagline=null; gate is precisely tagline membership — 0 tagline-bearing records excluded, 0 tagline-less included. (2) MARQUEE CONFIRMED: freighter/lobstr/blend/soroswap/reflector/stellarexpert/finclusive all have tagline=null yet trust={"label":"high","score":0.7}, freshness={"status":"fresh"}, desc 74–110 chars, absent from default — internally inconsistent as claimed. But per-query 0-result responses carry no signpost that matching records were suppressed, and the root cause stands: a missing cosmetic field contradicting the record's own trust/freshness labels and prose. (3) Decisive: ?q=identity%20verification&all=1 returns 5 results ranked runtime-verification(1), certora(2), veridise(3), finclusive(4), albedo(5) — the off-topic audit firms outrank BOTH on-topic identity partners even in the unfiltered set, so the WRONG-TOP is a ranker defect (token match on 'verification' in the formal-verification service), not merely quality-bar exclusion. This contradicts the API's own meta note ("relevance-ranked by fit... not exact-keyword text"). Tolerance fair: niche query, only 2 on-topic records exist, neither in top-5. Nuance: the secondary sub-claims (sector=identity → 0/0; q=kyc%20provider → 0 without all=1) are explained by the DOCUMENTED quality bar — both identity partners have tagline:null (albedo also no contact path), and the meta note says pass all=1 — so those parts are documented-but-misleading (EMPTY-DISHONEST flavor; root cause = missing taglines on finclusive/albedo) rather than WRONG-TOP. Headline WRONG-TOP label is correct.

confirmed.cell: PARTNERS × NON-RAMP lanes (audit firms, wallets, tooling, infrastructure, protocols, legal/agency, filters)

verdict.evidence: Reproduced 2026-07-09. (1) GET /api/partners?q=wallet → counts {"returned":3,"total":3}: anchor-coca-wallet (anchor), anchor-boss-pay (anchor), defindex (infrastructure) — zero partnerType=wallet records, exactly as claimed. (2) GET /api/partners?type=wallet → 0/0 even though "wallet" is in validTypes. (3) GET /api/partners?type=wallet&all=1 → 5/5: hana-wallet, albedo, xbull-wallet, freighter, lobstr — all published, all with EMPTY tagline and NO contactEmail/contactChannel, so all 5 fail the documented default quality bar ("Default results pass a directory quality bar (tagline + contact path, non-archived); pass all=1 for the unfiltered set" — quoted from meta.note). (4) GET /api/partners?q=wallet&all=1 → wallets rank 1,2,4,5,7 (hana, xbull, coca, freighter, lobstr, boss-pay, albedo, defindex, wallet-guru) — the claim's expected all=1 ranking is verified WORKING. (5) GET /api/partners?q=multisig%20wallet → same 3 non-wallets; lobstr absent despite its own description "Leading consumer Stellar wallet with LOBSTR Vault multisig signing"; with all=1 lobstr ranks 3. Verdict: the failure is real and reproducible, but it is not an out-ranking defect — the on-topic wallets are excluded upstream by the documented quality gate (their own records are thin), not beaten in a shared ranking, and the anchors shown legitimately contain "wallet" in their taglines. Harm is real: the category's 5 canonical records are invisible on the category's canonical query with a complete-looking total:3, and an explicit valid-type filter returns 0/0 while 5 published records exist. Fix path is data completeness (add tagline+contact to the 5 wallet records) or exempt/signpost type-filtered and type-matching queries from the quality bar.
verdict.correctedMode: RECALL-MISS (default lane; root cause = quality-bar gate excluding all 5 wallet records because they lack tagline+contact — a data-completeness gap), plus EMPTY-DISHONEST on type=wallet returning 0/0. Not WRONG-TOP: with all=1 the ranking is correct, so nothing off-topic outranks the wallets in a shared ranking.

verdict.evidence: Re-ran all probes 2026-07-09, all reproduce: /api/partners?q=dex → counts {returned:0,total:0}; ?q=dex&all=1 → soroswap ranked #1 ('AMM and DEX aggregator on Soroban — swap routing across Stellar liquidity'). ?q=amm → 0 vs all=1 → phoenix-protocol+aquarius+soroswap; ?q=liquidity → anchor-trace-finance only vs all=1 adds aquarius+soroswap; ?q=block%20explorer → 0 vs all=1 → stellarexpert. HOWEVER the mechanism is NOT a matcher recall failure: every excluded record has tagline:null, contactEmail:null, contactChannel:null, and the response's own meta.note documents 'Default results pass a directory quality bar (tagline + contact path, non-archived); pass all=1 for the unfiltered set' — the matcher recalls and ranks them all correctly under all=1. The failure survives verification on two record-provable grounds: (1) soroswap's own record says freshness.excludeFromMatching:false and trust:{score:0.7,label:'high'} — the system's own data marks it eligible for AI matching while the default matching lane hides it (internal contradiction); (2) the primary consumer, scout-mcp get_partners (scout-mcp/src/index.ts:645-668), exposes only type/sector/region/q with NO all param, so the note's all=1 signpost is unreachable for Raven — an agent asking for DEX/AMM/explorer partners gets a wrong-looking 0 and would conclude none exist, while the entire defi-protocol category (all 3 protocol partners) plus stellarexpert share the null-tagline/null-contact pattern and are categorically invisible by default.
verdict.correctedMode: EMPTY-DISHONEST (wrong-looking 0 results in the default/MCP lane; records retrievable and correctly ranked via all=1, so not a matcher RECALL-MISS) — root cause is a data gap: null tagline+contact on all defi-protocol and explorer partner records trips the documented directory quality bar, and the MCP tool cannot pass all=1

verdict.evidence: Re-ran probe: /api/partners?type=audit-firm&region=latam → {"counts":{"returned":0,"total":0}} while /api/partners?type=audit-firm → 5 firms (halborn, certora, runtime-verification, ottersec, veridise), all regions=['global']. Expectation verified fair on four grounds: (1) 'latam' is a real populated facet — 13 partners carry it (corpus regions: global 25, latam 13, africa 6, europe 3, asia 2, oceania 1, north-america 1), so the 0 is not an invalid-enum artifact; (2) the OpenAPI spec (/api/openapi.json) documents the param as "Filter by region served (global, latam, africa, …)" — a global firm serves LatAm, so literal array matching contradicts the documented contract, and the ramps param even advises "Combine with region/q for corridor lookups"; (3) the API's own q= lane implements the correct semantics: /api/partners?q=security%20audit%20for%20latam%20project returns all 5 global audit firms ranked 1-5 ahead of latam anchors — killing the 'strict facet by design' defense via internal inconsistency; (4) the zero is silent: meta lists validTypes and validRamps but no validRegions and the note never mentions global partners are excluded from specific-region filters, so an agent reads "no audit firms serve LatAm" — false by the corpus's own data. Label EMPTY-DISHONEST is correct (misleading false-zero from filter semantics; not RECALL-MISS since it's the filter lane, not ranking, and not DATA-WRONG since regions=['global'] is the right tag). Generalizes: every all-global type lane (audit-firm, and likely tooling/protocol) false-zeros on every specific region.

verdict.evidence: Re-ran all probes against live API 2026-07-09. (1) GET /api/partners?sector=banana → HTTP 200, no error, filter silently echoed: meta.filters={"sector":"banana",...}, counts={"returned":0,"total":0}; meta contains validTypes and validRamps but NO validSectors/validRegions. (2) GET /api/partners?region=mars → identical silent-accept, 200, 0 results. (3) Contrast within the same endpoint: ?type=lawyer → HTTP 400 {"error":"Unknown type 'lawyer'","validTypes":[...]}; ?ramps=banana → HTTP 400 {"error":"Unknown ramp 'banana'"}. So 2 of 4 enum-like filters validate, 2 don't. Refutation attempts failed: (a) tested whether sector/region are free-text (where any value would be legitimate and 0 honest) — they are not: enumerating all 47 partners (?all=1&limit=200) shows closed vocabularies, sectors={payments:29, defi:14, stablecoins:8, rwa:2, data:2, identity:2}, regions={global:25, latam:13, africa:6, europe:3, asia:2, north-america:1, oceania:1}; (b) tested whether the filter is functional at all — yes: ?sector=defi&all=1 → 14 results, ?region=latam&all=1 → 13 results, matching the enumeration exactly. Net: an agent typo'ing a sector gets a 200 with 0 results indistinguishable from a genuinely empty sector, and has no published vocabulary to self-correct against, while the sibling params prove the endpoint knows how to do this right. EMPTY-DISHONEST label is correct.

verdict.correctedMode: RECALL-MISS (label correct)
verdict.evidence: CONFIRMED, reproduced live 2026-07-09. (1) Probe re-run: GET https://stellarlight.xyz/api/builders?q=blessedux returns {"counts":{"returned":0,"total":0}} with advisory "No builders matched this query. The directory has 114 builder profiles... This is a filter miss". Same 0-result for q=wrever and q=miguelnietoa. (2) Ground truth from the records' own data: GET /api/builders?limit=200 returns all 114 builders; ALL five claimed usernames exist (blessedux, wrever, miguelnietoa, G0vermind, vanbarros76). blessedux's record: {"githubUsername":"blessedux","displayName":"Joaquin Farfan",...,"url":"https://stellarlight.xyz/builders/blessedux"} — githubUsername is the record's own primary key and its own url-field segment, so the query is provably implied by the record's data, not opinion. (3) q is a real supported text filter, not an ignored param: it is echoed in meta.filters, the API's own advisory names it ("broaden or drop a filter (q / location / skill)"), and q=Farfan → 1 result (blessedux), q=Joaquin → 2 results — proving q matches displayName but never githubUsername. (4) No correct-but-different lane excuses it: /api/builders/blessedux → HTTP 404 (no API detail endpoint; the url field is the HTML page), and ?username=blessedux is silently ignored (returns all 114). q is the only API lookup lane for a builder and it cannot match the primary key. (5) Fairness: exact-key lookup expects the record anywhere in results, not a contested ranking — 0 results at any K fails the most lenient tolerance. Bonus observed: q is also case-sensitive (q=Blessedux → 0 even against displayName-style matching), compounding the miss. Minor mitigation only: the advisory honestly says "filter miss, not an empty or unseeded directory", so it is not EMPTY-DISHONEST — but an agent doing known-item lookup still concludes the builder is absent when it is present, which is exactly RECALL-MISS.

(2) location=brasil → 2: "vanbarros76 | Rio de Janeiro, RJ, Brasil", "ceciliaromao | Brasil". The same 4 records are also missing from the latin-america expansion, so the miss propagates to the endpoint's advertised region queries. Caveat: the 2 brazil↔brasil misses are ironclad (same country name, spelling variant); the 2 city-only misses need city→country knowledge — slightly weaker but Rio de Janeiro is unambiguously Brazilian and geo-filtering is the endpoint's advertised job. confirmed.expected: 23 builders (14 Brazil-ish + 6 Chile + 2 Costa Rica + 1 Colombia per their own location fields) — the latam umbrella exists and expands country names, so it must include 'Brasil' and 'Rio de Janeiro' rows
confirmed.observed: 19 returned; the same 4 records missed by location=brazil (ceciliaromao, vanbarros76, CarolTea, ROAMONET) are also missing from the latam umbrella — its expansion vocabulary lacks 'brasil' and 'rio de janeiro'

verdict.evidence: Re-ran probe: GET https://stellarlight.xyz/api/builders?location=latam&limit=100 → "counts":{"returned":19,"total":19}. Diffed membership against ground truth: missing = exactly [CarolTea, ROAMONET, ceciliaromao, vanbarros76], unexpected = []. Ground truth verified from full corpus (GET /api/builders?limit=500 → 114 total): exactly 23 builders have LATAM location fields — 14 Brazil-ish (incl. ceciliaromao "Brasil", vanbarros76 "Rio de Janeiro, RJ, Brasil", CarolTea "Rio de Janeiro", ROAMONET "Rio de janeiro"), 6 Chile, 2 Costa Rica, 1 Colombia — matching the claim's 14+6+2+1=23 breakdown exactly. Umbrella fairness confirmed: none of the 19 returned locations contain the literal string "latam" (e.g. "Santiago, Chile", "São Paulo, Brazil", "Costa Rica", "Colombia"), proving the umbrella expands country names — so same-country records with local spellings are fairly implied. Cross-check: GET /api/builders?location=brazil&limit=100 → 10 returned (all containing English "Brazil"), missing the identical 4, confirming the shared expansion vocabulary lacks "brasil" and "rio de janeiro". Not a top-K issue (filter total=19, limit=100 > 23). Minor nuance: 2 of the 4 (CarolTea/ROAMONET) are city-only locations, so their sub-case is a city-vocabulary gap rather than a country-alias gap, but both are the same RECALL-MISS mode; the other 2 ("Brasil") fail even the narrowest country-name-expansion contract.

Meta echoes {"filters":{"q":"rust"}} — skill= is silently aliased to free-text q, and /api/builders?q=rust returns the identical 5 results in identical order, so no structured skill matching exists. A record with no Rust evidence outranks two records whose own bios explicitly list Rust — WRONG-TOP confirmed; no correct-but-different explanation (no skills field in builder records; match is a case-insensitive substring scan over the serialized record, and the skill→q aliasing is undisclosed, which is a secondary EMPTY-DISHONEST-adjacent contract issue).
