verdict.evidence: Re-ran both probes 2026-07-09. (1) /api/builders?location=south%20america&limit=100 → counts.total=0, builders=[]. (2) /api/builders?location=latam&limit=100 → 19 results. CONFIRMED and the expectation is fair on three grounds. FIRST, ground truth from the records' own location fields (full dump of all 114 builders): 21 builders are in South America — Brazil variants x14 ('Brazil' 5, 'São Paulo, Brazil' 2, 'Rio de Janeiro, RJ, Brasil', 'Nova Russas, Ceará, Brazil.', 'São Paulo, SP - Brazil', 'Brasil', 'Recife. Brazil. Earth.', 'Rio de Janeiro', 'Rio de janeiro'), Chile x6, Colombia x1 — so the >=20 expectation is met by the data itself. SECOND, region-umbrella matching is a supported feature class, not auditor taste: location=europe matches 'Arnhem, Netherlands' (1) and location=africa matches 'Kenya' (2), neither a literal substring, proving an alias-expansion layer exists; 'south america' is the same class of term and gets 0 while its near-synonym 'latin america' gets 19. THIRD, the OpenAPI description for getBuilders itself advertises 'Filter/find PEOPLE by region (Latin America, India, Africa, Asia, Europe)' — the contract promises region filtering, and 'South America' is a natural agent phrasing for exactly those records. One caveat: the claim's 'no hint... misleads an agent into concluding there are no South American builders' is overstated — the 0-result response includes an advisory: 'The directory has 114 builder profiles, but none match these filters — broaden or drop a filter (q / location / skill). This is a filter miss, not an empty or unseeded directory.' So the empty response is honestly framed (not EMPTY-DISHONEST), though it discloses no valid region vocabulary. Label RECALL-MISS is correct. Side observation while verifying (not this claim): even location=latam/brazil misses 4 Brazil-variant records ('Brasil', 'Rio de Janeiro, RJ, Brasil', 'Rio de Janeiro', 'Rio de janeiro') — latam's 19 = 17 SA + 2 Costa Rica, vs 21 true SA; the alias table also under-covers spelling/city variants.

confirmed.expected: Cluster size/scfFundedCount/scfTotalUSD should match the corpus the same API serves elsewhere: analyze?dimension=categories says User-Facing App=349 (scf 168, $16.9M), Protocol/Contract=131, Anchor=19, Asset=32; projects/search category totals agree (350/133/19/32; types: Stablecoin=30, Payments=246)
confirmed.observed: Every cluster is ~half the corpus: User-Facing App size=202 (scf 101, $9.08M), Protocol/Contract=88, Anchor=1 (only bitso), Asset=3 (ondo/redswan/spiko — usdc/brl/ars/eurs missing), types dimension Stablecoin=1 vs 30, Payments=124 vs 246. Sum of category clusters=500 vs 889 active projects. generatedAt is fresh but the underlying snapshot predates recent asset/anchor enrichment, and no scope note discloses a filtered universe (analyze DOES carry such a note). An agent asking saturated-vs-underbuilt gets materially wrong answers (stablecoins look wide-open at size 1; crowdedness scores computed off wrong sizes). Clusters is on Tyler's GQ hard-dependency list.

verdict.evidence: Re-ran all probes live (2026-07-09 ~18:09-18:12Z). /api/clusters category sizes: UFA=202 (scf 101, $9,084,889.4), Protocol/Contract=88, Tooling=109, Infrastructure=96, Asset=3, Anchor=1, Partner Integration=1 — SUM = exactly 500. /api/analyze?dimension=categories: totalProjects=889 active, UFA=349 (scf 168, $16.9M), P/C=131, Anchor=19, Asset=32, and it carries an explicit scope note; clusters meta discloses nothing ("filters":{"dimension":"category","minSize":1} only). /api/projects/search?category=X&limit=1 totals corroborate: Anchor total=19, Asset=32, UFA=350, P/C=133. Types dimension: clusters says Stablecoin size=1, Payments=124; paging the full corpus per-category through the same search API and counting the types field gives Stablecoin=28, Payments=140 — so Stablecoin 1-vs-28 is confirmed severe, but the claim's expected "Payments=246 / Stablecoin=30" was inflated (246 appears to be a q=payments keyword-match total, not a type count; actual types gap for Payments is 124 vs 140, mild). ROOT CAUSE pinned in source (/private/tmp/sl-canonical/src/app/api/clusters/route.ts ~line 189): payload.find({ collection: "projects", where: { status: { in: ["Development","Pre-Release","Live"] } }, limit: 500, ... }) — the route is force-dynamic and recomputes live per request, but hard-caps the fetch at 500 docs against an 889-doc active corpus; the exact-500 sum is the fingerprint. The dropped ~389 docs are systematically the newer records (recent asset/anchor enrichment), which is why it presents exactly like a pre-enrichment snapshot. Expectation fairness checks out: the endpoint's own doc header says it powers "most crowded / underbuilt category" questions across the directory with the same status filter analyze uses, and crowdedness is computed from the truncated sizes with no disclosed sub-universe — an agent gets materially wrong saturation answers (Anchor=1 vs 19, Stablecoin=1 vs 28).
verdict.correctedMode: STALE-SERVED is behaviorally defensible but mechanically imprecise — there is no cached snapshot; it's a live `limit: 500` fetch-cap truncation (DATA-WRONG on the served size/scfFundedCount/crowdedness aggregates, compounded by EMPTY-DISHONEST-style non-disclosure of the capped universe). Suggested label: DATA-WRONG (fetch cap), root cause limit:500 in src/app/api/clusters/route.ts.

Same authority-vs-topicality class as the known repo-search ranking defect: upstream non-Stellar activity is attributed to the Stellar ecosystem. Consider excluding upstream-dependency records from leaderboard ranking or flagging provenance.

verdict.evidence: Reproduced both probes 2026-07-09T18:09Z. (1) https://stellarlight.xyz/api/leaderboard (default sort=activity): Noir "rank":2, "github":{"totalStars":1374,"openIssuesTotal":619,"lastActivityAt":"2026-07-06T11:07:50.000Z"}. (2) https://stellarlight.xyz/api/leaderboard?sort=stars&limit=5: Noir "rank":2, above JavaScript Stellar SDK (692 stars), Rabet (644), Steepx (507); only the SDF org aggregate (6697) outranks it. Refutation attempts failed: /api/projects/search?q=noir shows the project's sole linked repo is noir-lang/noir (the upstream Aztec compiler; shortDescription's own text: "developed by Aztec Labs") and its stats are exactly the leaderboard numbers. Decisive corroboration from the corpus's own data: /api/repos/search?q=noir returns noir-lang/noir with "codeVerified":{"stellarProof":"none","codeDepth":0,"isDeployableContract":false} — the system's own scanner attests zero Stellar code linkage, yet those metrics drive ecosystem rank #2 on an endpoint the API reference describes as "Stellar ecosystem developer-activity stats and ranked project leaderboard" (with Electric Capital Stellar dev stats embedded in the same response). Not correct-but-different: Noir's directory membership is deliberate and fine; the defect is confined to the ranking surface. Nuance: default activity sort is a recency ordering (rank 1 = Microvault, 1 star), so the activity-sort instance is a near-daily upstream repo permanently squatting the default view rather than authority-weighting; the stars-sort instance is the clean WRONG-TOP. Same misattribution class as the known repo-search authority-vs-topicality defect, now on the leaderboard lane. Suggested fix direction (as filed): exclude or provenance-flag upstream-dependency records (e.g. stellarProof:"none" repos) in leaderboard ranking.

confirmed.expected: stats.outcomes should be exhaustive over the record's own totalSubmissions=39 (i.e. unknown=39 if untracked) or carry a scope label like analyze's funding.postHackathonStatusFunnel does ('hackathon-linked projects only (0 of 889 active)')
confirmed.observed: stats.outcomes = {built:0, inProgress:0, abandoned:0, unknown:0} alongside totalSubmissions:39 and winners:3, with no scope note — omission-reads-as-negation: an agent reads 'none of the 39 got built and none are unknown'. Root cause is corpus-wide: project hackathon/hackathonPlacement fields are null on all sampled projects (incl. hoops, whose Comet x Hoops Finance won 2nd at this event), so outcomes and clusters.hackathonWinnerCount are dead always-zero metrics on every record

verdict.evidence: Re-ran the probe: GET https://stellarlight.xyz/api/hackathons/stellar-hacks-blend serves "stats":{"totalSubmissions":39,"totalPrizeUSD":6000,"winners":3,"outcomes":{"built":0,"inProgress":0,"abandoned":0,"unknown":0}} with NO scope note — meta.note only scope-labels "submissions, winners, and tracks" as DoraHacks-sourced, not outcomes, so unknown:0 reads as "0 of 39 submissions have unknown status", which is false. Dead-zero confirmed corpus-wide: 5/5 sampled hackathon details (blend 39, zk 345, kale-reflector 46, paltalabs 27, build-on-stellar 84 submissions) all serve outcomes {built:0,inProgress:0,abandoned:0,unknown:0}. Root cause confirmed by the API's own data: /api/analyze?dimension=funding postHackathonStatusFunnel says "scope":"hackathon-linked projects only (0 of 889 active)" — zero projects in the corpus carry hackathon linkage; /api/clusters has 7 hackathonWinnerCount fields, all 0. The hoops counter-example holds: the blend record's own winners array lists "Comet x Hoops Finance" with hackathonPlacement "2nd Place" (github.com/hoops-finance/cometswap), yet /api/projects/search?q=hoops returns project slug "hoops" with hackathon and hackathonPlacement present-but-null — the linkage is provable from the corpus's own data, so DATA-WRONG (structured field false/missing vs reality) is a fair label, not adversarial opinion. Refutation attempt failed: the "outcomes is scoped to hackathon-linked corpus projects, so zeros are internally consistent" defense collapses because the response carries no such label, and the same API already implements the exact fix pattern (analyze's scope string), so the expected remedy (unknown=39 or a scope label) is precedented in-corpus. confirmed.expected: Consistent validation with the SAME endpoint's other enums: partners?type=bogus → 400 with validTypes, and meta ships validTypes+validRamps; unknown sector/region should 400 with a validSectors/validRegions list
confirmed.observed: HTTP 200 with 0 results and no valid-values list for sector (or region: partners?region=mars behaves identically); an agent probing sector values can't distinguish 'no partners in this sector' from 'not a real sector value'

verdict.evidence:
CONFIRMED — reproduced and strengthened; the inconsistency is real and the ambiguity is concrete, not hypothetical.

Re-ran probes (2026-07-09):
1. `/api/partners?sector=quantumfinance&limit=3` → HTTP 200, `"counts":{"returned":0,"total":0}`, meta ships `validTypes` and `validRamps` but NO validSectors/validRegions.
2. `/api/partners?region=mars&limit=3` → identical: HTTP 200, 0 results, no validRegions.
3. `/api/partners?type=bogus` → HTTP 400 `{"error":"Unknown type 'bogus'","validTypes":[...10 values]}`.
4. `/api/partners?ramps=bogus` → HTTP 400 `{"error":"Unknown ramp 'bogus'","validRamps":["on-ramp","off-ramp"]}`. So 2 of 4 enum-shaped filters on the SAME endpoint validate, 2 don't.

Fairness of expectation checked — it is ground-truth, not taste: `sectors` and `regions` are closed enums in the data model itself (Payload `select` fields with fixed options in /private/tmp/sl-canonical/src/collections/Partners.ts:322-332; SECTORS/REGIONS constants also duplicated in src/app/api/partners/submit-listing/route.ts:51 and onboard/route.ts:48). A validSectors list is trivially available in code. The route handler (src/app/api/partners/route.ts:225 `where.sectors = { contains: sector }`) simply skips validation for sector/region while type/ramps get the 400-with-list treatment.

Correct-but-different defenses tested and rejected:
- "Param silently ignored?" No — filter IS wired: `sector=payments` → 200 with total:16 real partners. So 0-results looks meaningful, which makes it worse.
- "Spec says free string, so 200-empty is contractual?" Weak: /api/openapi.json documents `type` with a full enum and `ramps` with an explicit "Unknown values return 400 with validRamps" promise, but `sector`/`region` as bare strings with a teaser list ("defi, payments, rwa, stablecoins, …") of what is actually a closed enum. The spec drift mirrors the code drift; it doesn't excuse it.
- Decisive indistinguishability probe: `sector=ai` and `sector=gaming` are VALID schema enum values with legitimately 0 partners in corpus (corpus sectors: payments 29, defi 14, stablecoins 8, rwa 2, data 2, identity 2). `sector=ai` (valid, empty), `sector=gaming` (valid, empty), and `sector=quantumfinance` (invalid) return byte-identical response shapes: 200 / returned:0 / total:0 / no validSectors. An agent enumerating sectors cannot tell "no partners serve AI" from "ai isn't a real value" — the exact EMPTY-DISHONEST failure.

Mode label EMPTY-DISHONEST is correct as filed. Fix surface (for the fixer, not this verdict): validate sector/region against the existing SECTORS/REGIONS constants → 400 with validSectors/validRegions (matching type/ramps), and/or ship validSectors+validRegions in meta alongside validTypes/validRamps; update /api/openapi.json to declare the enums.

Partially mitigated: the filters echo shows language:null / minScore:0, so a careful agent CAN detect it; scfAwarded=yes correctly coerces to true

verdict.evidence: Re-ran all probes 2026-07-09, claim fully reproduces. (1) Typo probe: /api/repos/search?q=sdk&langauge=Rust&limit=2 → HTTP 200, filters echo {"language":null,"minScore":0}, counts.total=248 (unfiltered); top result stellar/js-stellar-sdk with "primaryLanguage":"TypeScript". Control language=Rust → echo {"language":"rust"}, total=30, top result stellar/rs-soroban-sdk (Rust). Misleading effect is concrete: a typoing agent believes it filtered to Rust and receives a TypeScript repo as #1. (2) minScore=banana → echo "minScore":0, total=248, HTTP 200, no warning; limit=banana → silently coerced to 20. (3) scfAwarded coercion verified: yes→true (total 67, same as true), maybe→false (total 149, identical to omitting the param), exactly as claimed. Refutation attempts failed: (a) "standard REST silent-ignore" defense fails because the same API validates elsewhere — /api/analyze?dimension=bogus returns HTTP 400 {"error":"unknown dimension: 'bogus'","validDimensions":["all","hackathons","categories","funding"]} — so search lanes are inconsistent with the API's own established validation pattern; (b) the filters-echo mitigation (which the claim already acknowledged) is weaker than it appears: the echo for langauge=Rust (language:null) and scfAwarded=maybe (scfAwarded:false) is byte-identical to omitting the param, so detection requires the agent to diff the echo against its own request — the echo alone cannot signal "your param was not understood". Severity caveat: the echo is accurate about what WAS applied, so this is contract-hardening (agent-facing param validation), not data corruption. EMPTY-DISHONEST label is correct per the rubric, which explicitly includes "silently ignored param".

verdict.evidence: Re-ran both probes 2026-07-09 plus two refutation probes. (1) q=multisig&limit=10 → 7 results (counts.total=7): lobstr, stellarterm, ultra-stellar, bitgo, refractor, pipeline, centaurus. (2) q=multi-signature&limit=10 → 4 results (counts.total=4): solar-wallet, pakananet, teken, pakana. Expectation fairness confirmed from the records' own prose: lobstr's shortDescription says "LOBSTR Vault adds multisig" while solar-wallet's says "built-in multi-signature accounts" and teken's says "an easy-to-use multi-signature app for Stellar" — identical capability, so either spelling implies both records; there is also no types/tag lane for multisig (lobstr types=["Wallet"]) that could serve as the canonical route. Refutation attempts failed and widened the finding: (a) third spelling q=multisignature → a THIRD distinct set of 9 (stellarterm, ultra-stellar, bitgo, centaurus, blocknify, simple-signer, teken, zig3v2, hot-protocol) — notably it misses refractor, whose own description is "multisig signature aggregator for Stellar", i.e. the dedicated multisig-coordination service is invisible to two of the three spellings; union across spellings is ~13 projects, so any single spelling silently misses 30-70% of the multisig corpus. (b) semantic=true does not bridge it: response still reports meta.semantic:false, same 4 keyword results (this silent param ignore is a separate EMPTY-DISHONEST candidate, out of this claim's scope). Mechanism is visible in meta (matchMode:"strict","all keywords matched", via:"keyword") — strict keyword AND with no hyphen/abbreviation normalization — but mechanism-explains is not behavior-correct: this is the same synonyms/normalization class already patched elsewhere via the query-alias layer (#177), which lacks a multisig↔multi-signature↔multisignature alias.

Probe 1: https://stellarlight.xyz/api/projects/search?q=stellarexpert&limit=5 returns 3 results including BOTH copies: rank 1 slug='stellarexpert' (name "StellarExpert", types=[Explorer,Analytics], website https://stellar.expert/, github stellar-expert/stellar-expert-explorer, canonicalSlug=null, status=Live) and rank 3 slug='stellar-expert' (name "Stellar Expert", types=[Analytics], website https://stellar.expert/, github org stellar-expert, canonicalSlug=null, status=Live). Probe 2: https://stellarlight.xyz/api/projects/search?q=block%20explorer&limit=5 — both copies occupy ranks 4 AND 5 of the top-5, exactly as claimed. Adversarial checks all fail to refute: (1) Not two distinct products — the 'stellar-expert' record's OWN shortDescription reads "StellarExpert is a comprehensive block explorer and analytics platform for the Stellar Network...", i.e. it self-identifies as the same product as 'stellarexpert', and both share the identical website URL. (This is unlike AXIS, which is correctly a separate record for a different product by the same team.) (2) Not correct-alias behavior — the API exposes a canonicalSlug field precisely for aliasing, and it is null on both, so neither copy is marked as the duplicate of the other. (3) Expectation is fair — the query "stellarexpert" is implied by both records' own name fields, and a canonical entity should resolve to one record. Bonus corroboration of the types inconsistency: the [Analytics]-only copy's own prose calls itself a "block explorer" yet lacks the Explorer type, so the structured field contradicts the record's own prose (the prose-only-facts bug class). Severity is real in practice: the dup burns a slot in a crowded top-5 ("block explorer" total=5) and hands agents two conflicting type profiles for one entity. Side observation seen while probing (same class, separate finding): 'soroban-security-portal' and 'stellar-security-portal' both point to https://sorobansecurity.com/.

verdict.evidence: Re-ran all probes against live API. (1) /api/projects/search?q=decaf&limit=2 → shortDescription: "Decaf is a non-custodial wallet on the Solana and Stellar networks for cross-border money movement…" yet types=["Payments"] (no Wallet) and supportedNetworks=[] — exactly as claimed; defect provable from the record's own prose. (2) Fairness verified: 'Wallet' is a live type on 45 sampled projects and multi-label is the norm — identical-shape peers carry both (beans/coca/cypher/sentit/honeycoin = ['Wallet','Payments']), so Payments-only is not a defensible single-primary-category choice. supportedNetworks is genuinely populated elsewhere: 14 projects in a 549-project sample (lobstr ['stellar','xrpl'], allbridge/rozo/circle-cctp listing 'solana') — claim said ~25, I found 14 in a non-exhaustive sample; direction holds. (3) Retrieval consequence reproduces with mechanism: q=non-custodial+wallet (60 results, crowded → top-10 tolerance) puts decaf at #23; all top-34 results tie at keyword score 2, and the tie-break is Wallet-type-block first then prominence — lumexo (Wallet, prominence 0) outranks decaf (no Wallet, prominence 66). With the Wallet type decaf would land ~#8-9, inside top-10, so the missing field is directly retrieval-consequential. q=solana+wallet rank 10 also reproduces exactly. No correct-but-different explanation survives.
verdict.correctedMode: DATA-WRONG (label correct as filed)

confirmed.expected: securitize ('the world's largest tokenization platform', $4B+ AUM per its own record) and taurus ('custody, tokenization, and management' in own prose) in top-10 of 17
confirmed.observed: securitize rank 11, taurus rank 17 of 17 (last); the liqvid+liqvidxyz dupe pair consumes 2 of the top-10 slots (ranks 7-8), directly displacing on-topic records

verdict.evidence: Re-ran https://stellarlight.xyz/api/projects/search?q=tokenization&limit=20 (2026-07-09): 17 results, rankings reproduce exactly as claimed. (1) securitize at rank 11 — its own record reads "Securitize is the world's largest tokenization platform ... $4B+ in assets", the strongest possible own-data implication of this query, yet it sits outside top-10 on a 17-result crowded query. (2) The displacement is provable, not taste: ranks 7-8 are liqvid + liqvidxyz, a confirmed duplicate pair — identical shortDescription ("Liqvid.xyz is a compliant infrastructure layer powering tokenization..."), identical links (website https://liqvid.xyz/, github asterizm-protocol/asterizm-contracts-stellar, twitter @liqvid_xyz), neither carrying a canonicalSlug; deduping alone puts securitize in top-10. (3) Root cause visible in the response itself: score=1 and confidence.relevance=1 for ALL 17 results — zero topical differentiation; ordering is prominence/scfAwarded-first (dfns, types=['Security'], where "tokenization" appears only as a customer descriptor — "so banks, fintechs, and tokenization platforms can create...XLM wallets" — ranks #1 with prominence 80) then apparent insertion order. This matches the known authority-dominated ranking class. CAVEAT — the taurus half is the weak part of the claim: taurus's own prose lists tokenization as one of three services ("custody, tokenization, and management"), and all 16 records above it are dedicated tokenization/RWA records, so rank 17 is defensible ordering for a secondary-capability record and would NOT stand as a finding alone. The finding holds on securitize + the liqvid dupe pair; recommend filing it as WRONG-TOP (securitize displaced from top-10) compounded by a duplicate-record data defect (liqvid/liqvidxyz), and dropping taurus-at-17 from the evidence.
verdict.correctedMode: WRONG-TOP (confirmed for securitize; compounded by liqvid/liqvidxyz duplicate-pair data defect. Taurus sub-claim rejected as defensible ordering of a secondary-capability record)

confirmed.expected: wellspring's prose names payments/custody/yield, fundable's names automated payouts/payment streaming/offramping → types[] should carry at least Payments
confirmed.observed: Both records have types=[] (part of the 63 no_types records in the map). Prose keyword retrieval still works (both found in their probes), but any types-facet surface hides them entirely

verdict.evidence: Re-ran both probes (2026-07-09). (1) https://stellarlight.xyz/api/projects/search?q=treasury+management&limit=5 → wellspring at rank 4 with types=[] while its own shortDescription reads "institutional treasury management and payments platform enabling businesses to receive fiat and stablecoin payments, custody funds, earn yield (up to 12% APY), and move money globally"; neighbors in the same result set carry types (etherfuse ['RWA','Anchor'], bando ['RWA'], joona-pay ['Payments']). (2) https://stellarlight.xyz/api/projects/search?q=automated+payouts&limit=5 → fundable at rank 3 with types=[] while its prose reads "enabling automated payouts, payment streaming, and seamless crypto-to-fiat offramping... managing contributor payments at scale"; coinsender in the same set carries ['Payments','Anchor']. Fairness checks passed: 'Payments' is a real taxonomy value (directory-filters.tsx projectTypes list; carried by comparable User-Facing Apps joona-pay/coinsender/chipper/zarf/surgepay per ?q=payments&limit=10), and both records are category 'User-Facing App' like joona-pay, so empty types[] is omission, not a different-but-valid typing. Impact confirmed on a real facet surface: directory-projects-grid.tsx filters with baseWhere.types={in:typeValues}, and live https://stellarlight.xyz/directory?type=Payments renders 24 payments projects (afriex, beans, rozo, zebec...) with neither wellspring nor fundable present. Keyword retrieval still finding both (as the claim itself noted) doesn't refute DATA-WRONG — the failure mode is the structured field missing vs the record's own prose, which is exactly what reproduces. One nuance for the caller: the /api/projects/search endpoint itself has no type filter (?type=Payments is silently dropped from the echoed filters), so the facet exposure is the frontend directory and any types[]-consuming client, not the search API param — this refines the impact statement but does not weaken the finding. (Note: automated-finance, rank 1 on probe 2, is a third types=[] instance with treasury/payouts prose — same class.)

confirmed.expected: stellarfolio (own desc: 'portfolio tracking', types=[Analytics,Wallet]) in top-10 for the natural variant 'portfolio tracker'
confirmed.observed: stellarfolio ranks 11/11 on 'portfolio tracker' (just outside top-10) but 2/3 on 'portfolio tracking' — tracker/tracking not stem-unified while custody/custodial IS stemmed, showing inconsistent morphological handling

verdict.evidence: Re-ran both probes 2026-07-09. (1) /api/projects/search?q=portfolio+tracker&limit=12 → 11 results, ALL score=1, meta.matchMode="majority" ("majority of keywords matched (broader scope)") — i.e. the token 'tracker' matched ZERO records and the API fell back from strict AND; stellarfolio ranks 11/11, outranked by single-token 'portfolio' matches like Upwealth (types AI/DEX/Security/Bridge), Autowhale (SDK), Renesis (RWA), Cushion. (2) ?q=portfolio+tracking&limit=10 → strict mode, 2 results, stellarfolio 2/2 with score=2 (claim said 2/3; one-record corpus drift, same substance). Expectation is fair: stellarfolio's own shortDescription literally says "It provides features such as portfolio tracking, transaction history, and real-time balance updates", types=[Analytics,Wallet], status=Live — 'portfolio tracker' is the standard noun form of the record's own stated capability. With only 11 total results this is a niche query (top-5 tolerance), so 11/11 misses both top-5 and top-10. Stemming inconsistency verified: ?q=custody vs ?q=custodial return near-identical top-8 (lobstr, xbull, idos, slender, defindex, freighter, moneygram in both) — that pair IS unified while tracker/tracking is not. Class-wide, not record-specific: Kryptos (score=2 on 'tracking') also drops to score=1 on 'tracker'. Not excused by design: the majority-mode fallback is transparent, but the exact-concept record scoring 1 instead of 2 and ranking dead last behind off-topic generic-'portfolio' matches is a provable morphological-normalization gap (score 2→1 from the record's own prose). RECALL-MISS label stands.

verdict.evidence: Re-ran probe https://stellarlight.xyz/api/projects/search?q=mxlet&limit=1 — the API serves: "name":"MXlet","status":"Live","links":{"website":"https://xlet.io/","github":"https://github.com/mattpearce/xlet"}, sole repo "lastCommitAt":"2020-06-28T21:08:35.000Z", "lifecycle":null. Refutation attempt 1 (local DNS flake): queried public resolvers directly — dig @1.1.1.1 and @8.8.8.8 return NO A/AAAA records for xlet.io or www.xlet.io; zone has only NS/SOA at Cloudflare (an empty/parked zone), so this is a stable dead-site state, not a resolver or firewall issue. curl https://xlet.io/ and https://www.xlet.io/ both fail with '(6) Could not resolve host'. Refutation attempt 2 (project alive on another surface): GitHub API for mattpearce/xlet shows pushed_at=2020-06-28, 3 stars, not archived, and its own homepage field points at the same dead xlet.io — no alternate live surface exists; the record serves only these two links. Refutation attempt 3 (unfair expectation / 'stale repo ≠ defunct' rule): the primary evidence here is NOT repo staleness — it is that the record's own served website link has been removed from DNS entirely, directly contradicting the served structured field status=Live for a User-Facing App whose product page is the link. The 6-year-stale repo is corroborating only. An agent consuming this row would assert a defunct hardware wallet is Live. Mode label DATA-WRONG is correct (structured status field false vs the record's own served link and reality); matches the known defunct-projects-surface-as-active class with lifecycle=null unpopulated.

verdict.evidence: Re-ran probe https://stellarlight.xyz/api/projects/search?q=pluto-loans&limit=1 — row serves "status":"Live", "links.website":"https://plutoloans.com/", desc "Self-repaying loans: Spend your future yield today.", plus "lastActivityAt":"2022-08-01T15:24:10.000Z" and 5 repos all last-committed 2021–2022 (0 stars each). Independently verified the site: HTTPS fails with curl (35) "tlsv1 alert internal error"; HTTP serves a registrar parking page titled "Your domain is expired". Checked the domain-move counter-explanation via GitHub API: PlutoDAO org's newest push across all repos is loans-sdk at 2023-07-20 (~3 yrs stale), no repo homepage points to a replacement site — so it's a defunct project, not a stale link on a living one. Expired canonical domain = human-verifiable product death (stronger than the repo-staleness heuristic the project forbids acting on), so serving status=Live is provably false from the record's own served link. New instance of the known open defunct-surfaces-as-active class (no Inactive flag exists); pluto-loans is not in the previously flagged set. Corroborating oddity: row serves confidence.freshness:1 despite 2022 lastActivityAt.

confirmed.observed: sorosplits.xyz serves a 114-byte JS redirect to /lander which forwards to forsale.godaddy.com/forsale/sorosplits.xyz — domain is parked and for sale (GitHub repo findolor/sorosplits still exists; the website artifact is dead)
confirmed.cell: DATA-TRUTH spot-verify (random Live projects, website-liveness vs served status)

verdict.evidence: Re-ran all probes 2026-07-09. (1) API probe reproduces exactly: `curl "https://stellarlight.xyz/api/projects/search?q=sorosplits&limit=1"` returns the SoroSplits row with `"status":"Live"`, `"lifecycle":null`, `"links":{"website":"https://sorosplits.xyz/","github":"https://github.com/findolor/sorosplits"}`, `"verificationLevel":"Unverified"`, confidence label "high" (0.92, freshness:1). (2) Website is provably parked: `curl https://sorosplits.xyz/` returns HTTP 200 with a 114-byte body — exactly `<!DOCTYPE html><html><head><script>window.onload=function(){window.location.href="/lander"}</script></head></html>` — and following `https://sorosplits.xyz/lander` redirects to `https://forsale.godaddy.com/forsale/sorosplits.xyz?utm_source=TDFS_BINNS2&utm_medium=parkedpages...` (GoDaddy domain-for-sale lander; the 403 on the final hop is GoDaddy blocking curl UA, but the redirect chain with `parkedpages` UTM is definitive parking infrastructure). (3) GitHub repo findolor/sorosplits still exists (HTTP 200; API shows lastCommitAt 2024-10-20, isArchived:false), matching the claim's caveat. Fairness check passed: this is NOT the forbidden stale-repo-implies-defunct heuristic — a for-sale parked domain is direct evidence the served `links.website` artifact is dead, and the record itself serves that URL, so the defect is provable from the record's own data vs reality. Minor precision note for the fix: the incontrovertible defect is links.website pointing at a domain-sale lander while served with status=Live/high-confidence; whether the Soroban contracts remain usable on-chain is unproven either way, so remediation should be a human-verified status/lifecycle review + website-liveness flag, not an auto-demotion.

verdict.evidence: Probe reproduced exactly: /api/projects/search?q=borderdollar&limit=1 serves "status":"Live" and "links.website":"https://borderdollar.co/" (also malformed "twitter":"https://https/", "repos":[]). Both https://borderdollar.co/ and https://www.borderdollar.co/ return HTTP 404 with body title 'ConnectYourDomain Error | Wix.com' (Wix site disconnected). Refutation attempts failed: borderdollar.com is a GoDaddy parking lander (window._trfd.push({ap:"parking"}), wsimg parking-lander JS), not a relocated product site; borderdollar.io/.finance don't resolve; the row's GitHub link (github.com/randomacy = founder Jackie Tan's personal account) shows recent pushes only to unrelated personal repos (Pastepin, engram, jackietanyen.com-v2), no BorderDollar code; and Tracxn independently lists BorderDollar as deadpooled/inactive (Singapore, founded 2023). Not a staleness-heuristic overreach: the served website field itself hard-404s (provable from the row's own data) and status=Live is contradicted by multiple independent signals. DATA-WRONG label is correct; two concrete wrong fields: status=Live and links.website (plus junk twitter URL as corroborating hygiene defect).
